data.title: As Aave collapsed, Spark rose into the sky.
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

On April 18th, attackers exploited a vulnerability in the validator nodes of the Kelp DAO cross-chain bridge, releasing approximately $292 million worth of uncollateralized rsETH out of thin air. This was then deposited into Aave to lend out real WETH.

This batch of uncollateralized rsETH was accepted as compliant collateral, but the borrowed real WETH could not be covered by its equivalent value, resulting in Aave facing a bad debt exposure of up to $230.1 million.

According to on-chain analyst Yu Jin's monitoring, as of the morning of April 23rd, Aave's TVL had fallen below $30 billion, from $45.8 billion before the incident to $29.6 billion, with an outflow of $16.2 billion, a drop of over 40%.

The panic spread far beyond this. Protocols such as Morpho, Sky, and JupLend, which do not directly involve rsETH, also experienced large-scale outflows, and even protocols on Solana were not spared. However, this crisis also created an unexpected winner. Some of the funds fleeing Aave flowed directly to Spark, and the SPK token has risen by more than 150% in the past 7 days. 1. Aave Bears the brunt. To understand why Aave became the biggest victim of this event, we need to look at its structure. As the Liquid Restaking Token (RIT) of the Kelp DAO, rsETH's price movement is highly correlated with ETH, and it is classified by the Aave risk model as a high-quality collateral with low volatility and high liquidity. In E-Mode (Efficiency Mode), it enjoys a loan-to-value (LTV) ratio as high as 93%-95%, allowing users to borrow and re-borrow WETH with extremely high leverage. This parameter setting essentially prices the strong price correlation between rsETH and ETH, as well as its liquidity performance in the secondary market, but ignores rsETH's deep dependence on the LayerZero V2 cross-chain bridge contract. As a result, when attackers exploited a single DVN vulnerability in Kelp configuration on April 18th to mint approximately 116,500 uncollateralized rsETH (worth about $292 million) out of thin air, and deposited most of them (about 89,567) into Aave to lend out real WETH, the protocol functioned normally as designed, and neither the liquidation mechanism nor the oracle triggered any anomalies, but it was directly exposed to hundreds of millions of dollars in bad debt exposure. Ironically, just nine days before the incident (April 9th), the newly appointed risk service provider LlamaRisk submitted a Risk Stewards proposal, citing "strong market demand, ample on-chain liquidity, and healthy user leverage behavior," to increase the supply cap of Ethereum mainnet rsETH from 480,000 to 530,000. At this time, rsETH's supply utilization on Aave was close to 99.9%, and leveraged restaking strategies were in full swing, yet no one re-examined the security assumptions of the underlying bridging mechanism. After the attack, Aave quickly suspended WETH withdrawals, causing a significant discount to the deposit certificate aETHWETH. On-chain tracking shows that due to Aave's suspension of WETH withdrawals, the deposit certificate aETHWETH experienced a discount. A whale withdrew 13,000 ETH from the exchange, bought aETHWETH through swap, and then repaid the loan 1:1, netting a profit of 143 ETH. The contagion effect quickly spread to stablecoin pools. Circle's Chief Economist, Gordon Liao, initiated an emergency governance proposal suggesting raising the maximum deposit rate for USDC on the Aave V3 Ethereum mainnet to approximately 48.2% to address the near-paralysis of liquidity caused by USDC pool utilization exceeding 99.87% for several consecutive days. This situation is still developing. Aave previously proposed two loss handling paths: one is for all rsETH holders to jointly bear approximately 15% of the discount, resulting in bad debts of approximately $123.7 million; the other is to isolate the loss to L2, so that the Ethereum mainnet is unaffected, but Mantle will face a 71.45% WETH shortfall and Arbitrum will face a 26.67% shortfall, expanding the bad debt to approximately $230.1 million. On April 21, the Arbitrum Security Council announced the emergency freezing of 30,766 ETH (approximately $71 million) held by addresses associated with the attack, and the transfer of these assets to a governance-controlled intermediary wallet. Aave founder Stani Kulechov stated that the team is working with multiple partners to advance various recovery plans to achieve an orderly return to normal market conditions and protect user interests. The Arbitrum Security Council has recovered $70 million worth of ETH, significantly reducing potential risk exposure. DefiLlama founder 0xngmi further pointed out that if the frozen funds are prioritized for use in the Arbitrum local Aave market, bad debts on the chain could be reduced by 80% or even close to zero under the condition of rsETH sharing the impairment. Despite this, Aave's Umbrella security reserves (approximately $80 million to $100 million) still fall short of a potential gap of up to $230.1 million. The protocol has recommended suspending the Umbrella module to prevent automatic slashing from being triggered too quickly, and instead relying on governance for manual handling. As of now, the WETH reserves in the Ethereum Core V3 market have been partially unfrozen, but LTV remains at 0; the WETH reserves of chains such as Prime, Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea remain frozen or restricted. 2. Why did Spark avoid this loss? Spark achieved zero direct losses in the Kelp DAO rsETH bridging attack, a fact traceable back to January 2026. At the same time Aave incorporated rsETH into E-Mode (efficiency mode) and opened up high-leverage lending, Spark chose to delist assets with low usage rates, such as rsETH, while simultaneously tightening collateral eligibility standards. Following the incident, Spark's Head of Strategy, monetsupply.eth, explained the logic behind the move: Spark had long maintained a high maximum interest rate cap on the ETH lending market and had proactively ceded some business and revenue to Aave over the past year (the latter had previously lowered ETH borrowing rates to below 10% to attract leveraged users). This decision initially sparked strong dissatisfaction among ETH cyclical leverage users, with some funds flowing out of Spark. However, it turned out that delisting rsETH was an extremely prudent move—SparkLend's ETH withdrawal liquidity remained consistently ample, while the related markets on Aave were already experiencing high utilization and locked-up positions. monetsupply.eth also warned that ETH, as a core collateral, facing insufficient liquidity is not merely a user inconvenience, but a systemic security risk. He pointed out that in the current Aave environment, approximately 16.5% of the ETH market supply is supported by rsETH; if rsETH-related loans experience losses amortized across the mainnet and L2, E-Mode could face a 10%-15% reduction. This will prompt ETH suppliers to exit at an accelerated pace, resulting in utilization being locked at 100%, and lending rates failing to effectively incentivize the repayment of irrelevant LST to release liquidity. Therefore, if the price of ETH falls another 15%-20%, Aave may face significant bad debt accumulation. For Spark, this zero-loss outcome is also based on the premise that the market does not continue to decline. The Spark Protocol team stated in an interview with ChainCatcher that what allowed Spark to avoid the impact was not a single delisting action, but a more conservative risk parameter system. They pointed out that Spark used conservative LTV and strict rate-limited supply caps when onboarding rsETH, meaning that even without delisting, potential losses would be very limited and easily recoverable. “Any collateral listing implies a certain level of risk, and assuming losses will never occur is unrealistic. At SparkLend, we anticipate these kinds of events will happen occasionally and ensure the protocol is resilient to these scenarios.” The Spark Protocol team also revealed that they have multiple early warning tools, including AI detection and custom monitoring software. Upon learning of a potential attack, they completed a review of all direct and indirect exposures within 30 minutes and activated their market exit contingency plan. Regarding the large influx of funds after the incident, they stated that Spark can borrow billions of dollars from Sky at any time to meet any liquidity needs, and this high concentration of inflows is more of an endorsement of Spark Savings' security. Throughout the crisis, Spark Savings USDT was the only venue that maintained ample liquidity, with USDT liquidity never falling below $400 million. No one expected that Spark would be the biggest short-term beneficiary of this Aave bad debt crisis. After a large-scale exodus of funds from Aave, some flowed directly into Spark. Latest DefiLlama data shows that its TVL has rapidly increased from approximately $1.9 billion before the event to the $3.3-3.5 billion level (an increase of over 80%), partly attributed to continuous injections from large investors such as Justin Sun. Meanwhile, the SPK token price has seen a strong rebound: as of press time, SPK's 24-hour increase once exceeded 95%, and its 7-day increase exceeded 180%. F2Pool co-founder Wang Chun lamented that he received 83.7 million SPK rewards from Spark over the past year, which he sold on CoWSwap for 663 ETH and $1.4 million, and now he "has some regrets." However, as DefiLlama founder 0xngmi said, there are no real winners in these kinds of events. Spark's TVL growth is essentially a redistribution of existing DeFi funds among protocols, rather than new capital entering the market. The entire industry's "pie" is shrinking in the short term, and no one can remain unaffected. 3. Who should be held responsible? The entry point for this attack was the cross-chain bridge of Kelp DAO, configured as a single validator node in LayerZero. The lending protocol's smart contracts were flawless, but the losses ultimately fell on the lending protocol and its users. Crypto researcher CM pointed out a long-overlooked distinction: the cross-chain bridge version of the asset and the native asset are fundamentally different things. Just as bridged USDC is not equivalent to real USDC, the price of rsETH can be highly correlated with ETH, but its security is highly dependent on the reliability of the bridge contract. These are two completely different issues. As mentioned earlier, Aave's risk model accurately prices price correlation and liquidity, but systematically ignores the risks of bridging infrastructure. The problem doesn't stop there. Aave covers 22 chains, each with different bridging configurations, different oracle sources, and different liquidation paths. This complexity makes real-time monitoring by manual governance extremely difficult. When multiple chains experience problems simultaneously, the only thing the protocol can do is freeze the market, because there is no pre-designed mechanism to determine how losses should be distributed. Regarding liability, Kelp DAO and LayerZero are engaged in a public finger-pointing war: Kelp emphasizes that LayerZero's default configuration has vulnerabilities and that many protocols use similar settings; LayerZero, on the other hand, points out that Kelp chose a low-security single validator configuration, despite having previously recommended a multi-DVN solution. The two sides have yet to reach a clear agreement on the division of responsibility or a compensation framework. The latest data from Polymarket shows that the market's bet on Kelp sharing the losses has further decreased from 50% at the beginning of the incident to approximately 12%. A third perspective has also emerged in the community regarding the allocation of losses. A seasoned DeFi player, @DeFi_Dad (link: https://x.com/DeFi_Dad/status/2046376073932202076), asked in a discussion why both solutions involve users bearing the losses, instead of a "white knight" stepping in to fill the gap through a debt repayment plan. He specifically named Bybit, Coinbase, and Binance, believing this presents an opportunity for centralized exchanges (CEXs) to demonstrate their presence. Crypto investor @anndylian (link: https://x.com/anndylian/status/2046395943852093449) proposed a more specific solution: Kelp DAO would issue "debt certificates" to Aave, promising to gradually repurchase and burn uncollateralized rsETH with future staking commission revenue, turning a one-time hard de-pegging into installment repayment, thus preventing the Kelp token price from collapsing due to immediate redemption. Regarding the boundaries of responsibility, the industry consensus tends to be that Kelp DAO, as the issuer of rsETH, underwrote the configuration choices of the official LayerZero bridge; therefore, losses related to rsETH should primarily be borne by the entire rsETH holder community. Bad debts generated on Aave, on the other hand, should be the responsibility of Aave DAO for its risk management decisions (including rsETH's LTV, supply cap, and E-Mode settings). Ultimately, Kelp and LayerZero's blame-shifting highlights a current pain point in DeFi: in the complex system deeply intertwined with cross-chain, LRT, and lending protocols, once something goes wrong, no one can clearly define who should bear the responsibility; ultimately, it is often the lending protocol and its users who foot the bill. Despite Kelp DAO's latest updates signaling a "user-first" approach and active negotiation, the final outcome of the situation remains highly dependent on Kelp's handling of the remaining backed assets and the governance votes of all parties involved. This also confirms that, in the absence of industry consensus on the boundaries of responsibility, lending agreements must proactively internalize external dependencies such as bridging risks into their own risk control systems, rather than relying on post-event accountability or external bailouts.

data.title: $292 million KelpDAO cross-chain bridge stolen: Who should pay for it?
data.description:
On April 18, 2026, an attacker stole 116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million, from KelpDAO's cross-chain bridge in 46 minutes. This is the largest DeFi security incident so far in 2026. The stolen tokens were then deposited as collateral into lending protocols such as Aave V3, lending out approximately $236 million worth of ETH. This resulted in $177 million to $200 million in bad debt on the Aave platform, triggering a chain reaction affecting more than nine DeFi protocols. Aave's total value locked (TVL) evaporated by approximately $6 billion overnight. The details of the incident have been widely reported and will not be repeated here. In fact, the author himself has tens of thousands of USDT that he cannot withdraw... which is why the author is very motivated to conduct research. This article will explore a different question: From a civil law perspective, who should bear the responsibility? Can the victims actually receive compensation? The answer is far more complex than the initial finger-pointing within the crypto community. After a systematic analysis of the applicable legal framework, I believe that KelpDAO and LayerZero Labs bear concurrent liability, roughly 60% KelpDAO and 40% LayerZero; furthermore, the capped liability clauses in the terms of service of both agreements are almost certainly unenforceable. The core liability issue: two failures, one attack. Discussions surrounding this attack always begin with the same argument: was it KelpDAO's fault (choosing a 1-of-1 DVN configuration), or LayerZero's fault (its DVN's RPC infrastructure was poisoned)? The answer is: both are responsible.

(I) What KelpDAO Did Wrong

LayerZero's cross-chain messaging protocol uses a decentralized verifier network (DVN) to verify the authenticity of messages sent from one blockchain to another. The protocol is designed to be highly flexible: each application deployed on LayerZero can choose how many DVNs are needed to reach consensus before a message is trusted. LayerZero's own documentation recommends at least a 2-of-3 configuration, meaning that a message is only accepted after at least two out of three independent validators have confirmed it.

KelpDAO chose the absolute minimum configuration: 1-of-1. One validator. Zero fault tolerance.

KelpDAO chose the absolute minimum configuration: 1-of-1. One validator. Zero fault tolerance.

... This means that anyone who can break, cheat, or manipulate this single validator can forge arbitrary cross-chain messages, including one instructing KelpDAO's bridge to release all of its rsETH reserves to an attacker-controlled address. And this is exactly what happened. This is quite absurd: KelpDAO's bridge locks up approximately $1.6 billion in value across more than twenty blockchain networks. The protocol chose to protect these assets with a single point of failure, akin to using a padlock to protect a bank vault, while the manufacturer explicitly recommends at least a three-lock system. Within the framework of traditional tort law, this analysis is quite straightforward. The Restatement (Second) of Torts defines negligence as conduct that falls below the standard of care established by law to protect others from unreasonable risk. [1] For professional actors, protocol operators who manage billions of dollars of user assets undoubtedly fall into this category, and the standard of care should be raised to the level of skills and knowledge that practitioners in this industry usually possess. [2] The most classic risk utility analysis framework was proposed by Judge Learned Hand of the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit in United States v. Carroll Towing Co. [3]: If the cost (B) of preventive measures is less than the probability (P) of damage occurring multiplied by the size of the damage (L), then failure to take preventive measures constitutes negligence. That is: when B < P×L, failure to take preventive measures is negligence. In this case, there is no doubt about this equation: P (probability): Cross-chain bridge attacks are one of the most common and most damaging types of attacks in DeFi. Wormhole ($320 million, 2022), Ronin ($625 million, 2022), Nomad ($190 million, 2022), and Drift Protocol ($285 million, April 1, 2026, just 17 days before this attack) all demonstrate that bridging security is a known and active threat. L (Scale of Damage): $292 million in direct losses, plus hundreds of millions of dollars in cascading bad debts for downstream protocols. B (Prevention Costs): Changing the bridge's DVN configuration from 1-of-1 to 2-of-3. Additional costs: Minor verification delays (a few seconds) and DVN fees (negligible relative to the value of the assets being protected). No rational protocol operator could justify using a 1-of-1 configuration for assets of this scale. The cost of prevention is negligible, while the anticipated damage is catastrophic. It's worth noting that the practices of industry peers provide important references. SparkLend sets the loan-to-value (LTV) ratio for rsETH at 72%, and Fluid at approximately 75%, both significantly lower than Aave's 93%. This conservative approach may reflect the industry-wide awareness of the underlying bridging risks of rsETH. If even lending protocols are wary of the bridging risks of rsETH, then KelpDAO, as the operator of the bridge itself, should bear a higher standard of security. However, the opposite is true: the party operating the bridge chose the minimum security configuration. Another important argument needs to be discussed: the on-chain transparency argument. The 1-of-1 DVN configuration is publicly verifiable on-chain data; any technically capable user can verify the bridge's security parameters by querying the LayerZero EndpointV2 contract. KelpDAO might argue that since the configuration is public, users have the opportunity (and responsibility) to assess the bridge's security before depositing assets. This constitutes a de facto assumption of risk defense, distinct from contractual waivers of service terms (analyzed in Part II). The strength of this defense depends on how the court views the "reasonableness" standard of DeFi users—can ordinary DeFi users be expected to vet the bridged DVN configuration before depositing assets? This defense may be effective for institutional users and highly skilled "whales"; its persuasiveness is greatly reduced for ordinary retail investors.

(II) What Did LayerZero Do Wrong

However, KelpDAO's configuration choice itself was not sufficient to cause loss. This attack also required the attacker to deceive LayerZero's DVN into signing and verifying a transaction that never occurred. It is at this stage that LayerZero's legal risks become clear.

According to a detailed analysis published by Cos (Yu Xian), founder of the well-known blockchain security company SlowMist, [4] this attack was not a cracking of the DVN key or an exploitation of the LayerZero protocol logic. The attackers targeted the upstream data source of DVN: the RPC nodes used by DVN to read the blockchain state. The attack was carried out in five steps: The attackers obtained the list of RPC nodes used by LayerZero DVN. The attackers compromised two independent RPC node clusters and replaced the legitimate `op-geth` binary with a version containing a Trojan. The trojanized binary employs selective spoofing: it only returns forged data to requests from DVN IP addresses. All other IP addresses, including LayerZero's own Scan monitoring service, receive the real data. This IP-based selective response pattern makes the poisoning completely invisible to regular monitoring. The attacker launches a DDoS attack on the uncompromised RPC node, forcing DVN to failover to the infected node. After the forged verification is complete, the malicious binary self-destructs and wipes all logs, eliminating forensic evidence. This is crucial: LayerZero operates this DVN. This is not a passive software repository deployed by KelpDAO. LayerZero actively runs the verification infrastructure, selecting RPC providers, configuring failover logic, and signing verification certificates. When DVN reads a forged on-chain state from a poisoned RPC node and signs and verifies a non-existent transaction, it is LayerZero's infrastructure that is failing. Moreover, this attack vector is not new. As Cos points out, "RPC poisoning attacks are an old trick that exchanges have been using for years." [5] According to the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), an actor must recognize the risks that a rational person in his position would recognize. [6] RPC poisoning is a documented attack category in the blockchain security community. A reasonable infrastructure provider operating DVN to protect billions of dollars of cross-chain assets should have implemented countermeasures, including: (a) dispersing RPC sources across multiple independent providers and geographic locations; (b) implementing cross-validation among RPC nodes to detect data inconsistencies; (c) monitoring IP-based selective response patterns; (d) hardening failover logic to avoid falling back to untrusted nodes under DDoS pressure; and (e) implementing anomaly detection for DVN verification requests (e.g., flagging unusually large transaction amounts). Furthermore, the non-delegable duty doctrine also applies to this case. According to the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), certain security-critical functions cannot be fully delegated to a third party, and the party undertaking such a duty has a responsibility to ensure its full performance. [7] When LayerZero claims to provide verification infrastructure for high-value cross-chain transactions, it cannot evade responsibility by pointing to RPC providers as independent contractors. LayerZero selected these providers, configured failover logic, and operated verification nodes. The responsibility lies with the operator. A comparable traditional legal concept is the responsibility of financial infrastructure operators. SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) provides messaging infrastructure for global interbank communications. If SWIFT's message verification system is compromised, leading to the execution of fraudulent transfer instructions, SWIFT cannot be absolved simply because "the protocol itself has no vulnerabilities." It operates the verification infrastructure, and this operation itself carries a duty of care commensurate with the value it protects. LayerZero's role in the DeFi ecosystem is highly similar: it is not merely a software licensor, but the operator of cross-chain message verification infrastructure. The constructive notice effect of the Drift Protocol attack also needs to be considered. On April 1, 2026, Drift Protocol suffered a $285 million cross-chain attack, just 17 days before the KelpDAO attack. While the specific attack vectors of the Drift attack may differ from those in this case (this requires further verification), it sends a clear signal to the entire cross-chain infrastructure industry: cross-chain bridging infrastructure is under active attack by advanced persistent threats (APTs). In this context, LayerZero, as one of the largest cross-chain messaging protocols, should have been on high alert. Its failure to strengthen the security of its RPC infrastructure after the Drift attack further supports the finding of negligence. LayerZero's strongest defense is the complexity of the state-sponsored attack. The combination of binary file replacement, IP-based selective spoofing, DDoS forced failover, and post-attack self-destruction represents an unusual level of operational complexity, potentially approaching the level of the SolarWinds supply chain attack. According to Section 302B of the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), the risk posed by highly unusual criminal intervention is beyond reasonable prevention. If the court determines that the complexity of this attack exceeded the standard of reasonable care for a private sector infrastructure provider, LayerZero's liability for negligence could be significantly reduced or even eliminated. However, the arguments against this defense are equally compelling: as Cos points out, the individual components of this attack are well-known, even if their combination is novel. RPC poisoning is a known technique. DDoS is a known technique. Binary file replacement is a known technique. A reasonable infrastructure operator should defend against these known individual threats, even if it cannot foresee the exact combination of these threats. (III) Concurrent Causation and 60/40 Fault Sharing This is a classic case of concurrent causation. KelpDAO's 1-of-1 configuration and LayerZero's failed RPC infrastructure were both necessary conditions for the attack's success. Removing any one of them would cause the attack to fail: If KelpDAO uses a 2-of-3 standalone DVN (with a standalone RPC infrastructure), the attacker would need to compromise multiple standalone authentication paths simultaneously, which would greatly increase the cost and complexity of the attack. If LayerZero's DVN is not deceived by poisoned RPC data, the 1-of-1 configuration itself works normally, and no unauthorized messages are verified. According to the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), when two or more causes contribute to a single indivisible damage, each is considered a "substantial factor" of the damage, and each tortfeasor is liable. [8] The attacker's criminal act does not break the causal chain because bridging attacks against a single point of failure are precisely the foreseeable risks that the multi-DVN recommendation aims to prevent. [9] New York and California, the most likely jurisdictions for any such lawsuit, employ a pure comparative fault regime. [10] This means that each defendant's liability is reduced proportionally to their fault, but not entirely absolved. So how is fault allocated? I estimate it to be approximately 60% for KelpDAO and 40% for LayerZero, for three reasons: First, KelpDAO made a proactive choice to opt for 1-of-1 when LayerZero explicitly recommended using at least 2-of-3. This was a governance decision, not a technical constraint imposed by LayerZero. The protocol had the capability to choose higher security but did not. This proactive choice carries significant weight in any comparative fault analysis. Second, the 1-of-1 configuration is a fundamental prerequisite for an attack to be carried out. Without it, the attacker would face a fundamentally different (and much more difficult) challenge. The RPC poisoning attack succeeded simply because there was only one verification path to compromise. A multi-DVN configuration with independent infrastructure creates defense in depth, making this attack unbreakable. Third, however, LayerZero cannot be blameless. LayerZero operated the DVN whose infrastructure was compromised. RPC poisoning is a known attack vector. The Drift Protocol attack 17 days ago put the entire cross-chain industry on high alert. While LayerZero's own defense that "the protocol was not compromised" is technically accurate at the protocol level, it masks the fact that LayerZero's operational infrastructure was a direct tool of the loss. The 40% allocation to LayerZero reflects the reality that it operated flawed infrastructure, used an architecture with known vulnerabilities, and failed to implement standard countermeasures against documented attack categories. Can the Terms of Service save them? Both KelpDAO and LayerZero maintain Terms of Service ("ToS") with extremely aggressive liability limits. KelpDAO caps its total liability at the greater of the amount paid in the first twelve months or $200. [11] LayerZero caps its liability at $50. [12] Both include standard "as is" (AS IS) disclaimers and broad assumption of risk clauses. If these caps are in effect, all of the above civil liability analysis would be meaningless. A $200 cap on a $292 million loss would render KelpDAO virtually immune to any meaningful recourse. These caps would not be upheld by the courts. The reasons are as follows.

(I) The principle of manifest unfairness

Contract law has long recognized that some terms are so fundamentally unfair that courts will refuse to enforce them. The principle of manifest unfairness has been explicitly stated in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which allows courts to revoke contract terms that are both procedurally and materially manifestly unfair. [13]

Procedural unconscionability examines whether there is a meaningful opportunity to negotiate or refuse the terms. DeFi terms of service are typical adhesion contracts: presented in an either-or manner with no opportunity for negotiation, and are usually buried deep in a website that most users never visit. Most DeFi users interact directly with smart contracts through wallet interfaces such as MetaMask, and they never browse the website of the protocol, let alone read or agree to multi-page terms of service documents. The legal distinction between "clickwrap" and "browsewrap" has been established. [14] In "Specht v. Netscape" [15], then-Second Circuit Court of Appeals Judge Sotomayor (now a U.S. Supreme Court Justice) ruled that a hyperlink to the terms of service below a download button is insufficient to constitute user consent if it is not prominently displayed. In "Nguyen v. Barnes & Noble" [16], the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals also ruled that websites must provide prominent notice and the opportunity to review the terms; simply using the website itself is not enough. DeFi protocol interactions are closer to the situation in the Spetht case than in Meyer v. Uber [17] (in which a prominent registration page with a clear hyperlink to the terms was ruled a valid notice). Whether on-chain smart contract interactions constitute consent to off-chain website service terms has not yet been positively decided by any court, but the weight of existing browser case law strongly discourages the enforcement of terms in the absence of user affirmative action. Substantive unconscionability examines whether the terms are so one-sided as to be “shock the conscience.” The $200 liability cap to $292 million in losses, a ratio of approximately 1:1,460,000, is a textbook example of substantive unconscionability. LayerZero’s $50 cap ratio is even more extreme. In the landmark case of Williams v. Walker-Thomas Furniture[18], the court established that it would not enforce terms that are “unreasonably favorable to the drafter” when the other party has no meaningful alternative. The commentary in the Restatement of Contracts confirms that “gross disparity in the exchange” is direct evidence of unconscionability. [19]

（ii） Gross Negligence Exception

Even if a court finds that the terms of service are enforceable at a general level, limitation of liability clauses do not protect against gross negligence or willful misconduct. This is an established principle in New York and Delaware law.

The Restatement of Contracts (Second Edition) states that clauses that exempt a party from liability for torts caused by reckless or willful conduct are unenforceable because they violate public policy. [20] The New York Court of Appeals has repeatedly affirmed that exemption clauses do not cover gross negligence and apply the "reckless disregard" standard. [21]

Does KelpDAO's 1-of-1 DVN configuration constitute gross negligence? The arguments are strong.

Gross negligence requires a reckless disregard for known significant risks, going beyond mere insufficient care. KelpDAO chose the minimum security configuration for a bridge protecting over $1 billion in assets, defying the infrastructure provider's explicit recommendations. The risk of a single point of failure being breached is well documented. The difference between 1-of-1 (zero tolerance) and 2-of-3 (33% tolerance) is not a difference in marginal risk, but a fundamental one. If the court characterizes the 1-of-1 choice as recklessness rather than mere negligence, then the $200 cap will be invalid regardless of the outcome of the unfairness analysis. The gross negligence exception is important because it bypasses the threshold of controversy surrounding the validity of the terms of service. Even if the court finds that the user did indeed agree to the terms of service (e.g., through a clickwrap mechanism), and even if the court finds that the $200 cap does not constitute unfairness in a general business context (e.g., for institutional users), the gross negligence exception still applies independently. It is a public policy doctrine and is not bound by the agreement of the parties. Under New York law, this doctrine has been repeatedly affirmed,[21] forming the most robust second line of attack against the terms of service.

(iii) Veto Power of Securities Law

There is a third path to invalidate the caps of services, and it is the most powerful.

If rsETH is classified as a security under federal law, then the caps of service and the arbitration clauses will be invalid by operation of law. The Securities Act states that "any condition, provision or agreement that binds any person to waive any provision of this Act" is invalid. [22] The Exchange Act contains the same anti-waiver clause. [23] These clauses cannot be circumvented by contract. They take precedence over the Federal Arbitration Act. They are not subject to state unfairness analysis. They are mandatory orders of federal law. Does rsETH meet the definition of a security? According to the basic test established in the Howey Test [24], an investment contract is valid if: (1) money is invested, (2) it is a joint venture, (3) a profit is expected, and (4) the profit comes from the efforts of others. rsETH meets each of these requirements. Users deposit ETH (money investment) into a collective restaking strategy on EigenLayer (joint venture). rsETH generates income through restaking rewards (expected profit). The restaking strategy, operator selection, and bridging infrastructure are entirely managed by the KelpDAO team, and individual holders have no control over them (the efforts of others). The complexity lies in the split holding ruling in the Ripple case. [25] In 2023, the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York distinguished between direct institutional sales (which are securities) and programmatic secondary market sales on the public market (which are not securities). Most rsETH transactions occur on the secondary market, through DEX swaps and Aave deposits, rather than through direct purchases from KelpDAO. Under the Ripple framework, secondary market buyers may not meet the requirement of "efforts of others." However, Ripple is only a district court ruling, currently appealed to the Second Circuit, and its applicability to liquid staking tokens has not yet been tested.

If the securities classification is successful, it will completely change the entire recourse landscape. The cap on the terms of service will disappear. The arbitration clause will disappear. Direct purchasers will gain private rescission rights. [26] All purchasers who rely on KelpDAO's claims regarding the security of the bridging can file fraud claims. [27]

It is important to explain the power of this legal tool here: Under US law, arbitration clauses and class action waivers are generally strongly protected. The Supreme Court established the federal arbitration law's prerogative over state law in AT&T v. Concepcion[28] and Epic Systems v. Lewis[29] to negate class-action waiver clauses in arbitration agreements. In American Express v. Italian Colors[30], the Supreme Court further narrowed the "effective vindication doctrine," ruling that an arbitration clause could only be overturned if it prevented the assertion of a statutory right, and that high litigation costs alone were insufficient grounds for overturning it. This means that if LayerZero's arbitration clause were valid, it would force victims to pursue individual arbitrations with a cap of $50 per person, which would functionally be a complete liability barrier. No rational plaintiff would initiate individual arbitration proceedings for $50 in damages. However, anti-waiver provisions in securities law provide a way to circumvent this barrier. If rsETH is a security, federal law directly rejects the arbitration clause and the class action waiver clause, without invoking the doctrine of unfairness or challenging the priority of the Federal Arbitration Act. This is why the security classification is the most critical “nuclear weapon” in the whole analysis.

RPC Providers: Supporting Roles

The RPC node providers whose infrastructure was poisoned are in a special position in this chain of responsibility. They provided the false data that DVN relied on. But their liability is limited by several factors.

According to the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), a supplier who provides information in the course of business is liable for economic losses caused by reasonable reliance if they fail to exercise reasonable care, but only to a foreseeable “limited group” that the supplier intended to reach or knew that the recipient intended to reach. [31] In New York State, Credit Alliance v. Arthur Andersen [32] further limited the liability of information providers to third parties with a three-factor test. In this case, the RPC provider's liability may only extend to LayerZero (who directly chooses and relies on them), and not to downstream KelpDAO users or rsETH holders. This means the RPC provider's liability is primarily a contribution claim to LayerZero, a mechanism by which LayerZero transfers its 40% share of fault downstream, rather than a direct avenue for recourse for the victims. There is also a practical obstacle: the RPC provider's identity has not been publicly disclosed. They themselves may be victims of state-sponsored cyberattacks, the sophistication of which (binary file replacement, IP-based selective spoofing, DDoS, self-destructing binaries) suggests operational capabilities beyond those of ordinary cybercriminals. If the provider itself is a victim of a state-sponsored attack, its negligence is difficult to establish; note that standards do not require ordinary commercial entities to defend against military-grade intrusions. Most likely outcome: The RPC provider's responsibility remains behind the scenes, possibly related to the debt recovery process between KelpDAO and LayerZero, but not the primary path to recovery for victims. Aave's 93% Loan-to-Value Ratio: A Fiduciary Duty Issue. The attack stole $292 million from KelpDAO's bridge. But the contagion effects—$177-200 million in bad debt, a $6 billion drop in TVL, and principal losses for depositors—were amplified by Aave's governance decisions. (I) Aggressive Parameter Settings In January 2026, Aave governance passed Proposal 434, increasing the e-mode loan-to-value (LTV) of rsETH from 92.5% to 93%. This means that for every $100 of rsETH collateral, a user can borrow $93 of ETH. In comparison, Aave's competitors: SparkLend sets the rsETH LTV at 72%, and Fluid at approximately 75%. The difference is not small; the 21 percentage point difference reflects fundamentally different risk philosophies. At a 93% LTV, the margin of safety is only 7%. Any drop in collateral value exceeding 7% will result in bad debts, which will be borne by Aave's depositors (not borrowers). For a collateralized asset whose value depends on a cross-chain bridge with a single point of failure, a 7% safety margin is objectively insufficient.

(II) Legal Framework: DAO as a General Partnership

The legal landscape of DAO governance responsibilities has changed dramatically in the past two years.

In Samuels v. Lido DAO[33], a California federal court ruled in 2024 that Lido DAO had reasonable grounds to be considered a general partnership under California law. Token holders participating in governance may be considered general partners and bear personal liability for partnership obligations. In Sarcuni v. bZx DAO[34], another California federal court reached a similar conclusion, ruling that DAO token holders participating in governance were jointly and severally liable. Under California’s Revised Uniform Partnership Act (RUPA), partners have fiduciary duties of care and loyalty to one another,[35] and are jointly and severally liable for all partnership obligations.[36] (iii) Caremark’s Duty of Oversight[37] Delaware’s fiduciary duty framework, analogous to DAO governance, provides the most relevant standard of care. In the landmark case of Caremark[37], the court established that trustees have a positive duty to establish and monitor compliance and risk management systems. Stone v. Ritter[38] affirmed that Caremark’s duty of oversight requires proof that the trustee either (1) completely failed to establish a monitoring system or (2) established such a system but consciously failed to pay attention to its outputs, and that such conscious indifference constitutes bad faith. Aave's situation falls into the second category. Aave did not lack a risk management system; it had employed Chaos Labs for three years. But on April 6, 2026, Chaos Labs publicly left, its founders citing "fundamental disagreements on risk strategy." [39] Twelve days later, the attack occurred. This coincidence is extremely compelling in terms of evidence: Aave's risk manager left due to strategic disagreements, and within two weeks, the precise risk category amplified by the aggressive LTV parameter, collateral value collapse, was realized on a catastrophic scale. According to the "Van Gorkom case," [40] the business judgment rule is overturned when directors approve major decisions without adequate information. If Aave governance did not conduct any assessment of the rsETH bridge security when approving 93% of the LTV, and was particularly unaware that the rsETH bridge relied on zero-fault-tolerant 1-of-1 DVN, this is precisely the kind of uninformed decision that Van Gorkom targeted. (iv) Practical Limitations The fiduciary duty doctrine for Aave governance is legally sound but practically limited. Anonymous governance voters are not recourse; you cannot collect restitution from anonymous wallet addresses. The Lido DAO case law itself is still in active litigation (the dispositional motion is scheduled for hearing in November 2026) and may be overturned. However, not all governance participants are anonymous. Institutional delegates—venture funds, protocol treasuries, and professional governance services—are identifiable and personally liable under the Lido/bZx partnership framework if they voted in favor of Proposal 434. The doctrine can be applied to these identifiable delegates. Here, it's necessary to explain why the Caremark obligation is so crucial. In traditional corporate law, the Caremark obligation represents a minimum level of oversight responsibility. Directors are not required to micromanage the company's operations, but must ensure the existence of a reasonable information reporting and compliance monitoring system. Directors breach the Caremark obligation when they either (1) fail to establish such a system entirely, or (2) have established one but consciously ignore its warning signs. In the context of Aave, this obligation has a very specific meaning: Did Aave governance examine the security architecture of the underlying rsETH bridge when accepting rsETH as collateral? Specifically, were any governance participants, risk committee members, or delegators aware that the rsETH bridge relied on a 1-of-1 DVN configuration, i.e., zero fault tolerance? If the answer is no (which is very likely in the current loose practices of DeFi governance), then Aave governance set a safety margin of only 7% for billions of dollars worth of collateral without fully understanding the risks. This is precisely the "uninformed decision-making" targeted in the Van Gorkom case. Furthermore: Chaos Labs' departure is more than a coincidence. Having served as Aave's risk management provider for three years, Chaos Labs was intimately familiar with Aave's risk framework. When its founders publicly acknowledged "fundamental disagreements on risk strategy," this constituted a significant warning sign. A prudent governance system should immediately review risk parameters after a risk management provider leaves due to strategy disagreements, or at least suspend the listing of new high-risk collateral, especially given the recent Drift Protocol attack. Aave governance did not take any such action. The deeper implications of Aave's governance issues are systemic. If DeFi governance voters can be held personally accountable for risk parameter decisions that amplified attack losses, this would fundamentally change how governance participants treat collateral listings and LTV parameters. The fact that 93% of LTV was used to bridge assets at a single point of failure could become a classic case of governance negligence, a DeFi version of the Caremark red flag. A noteworthy timeframe: The dispositive motions in Samuels v. Lido DAO are scheduled for hearing in November 2026. If the California federal court confirms that Lido DAO constitutes a general partnership and that governance token holders bear personal liability, this will clear the legal hurdle for similar lawsuits against Aave governance participants. Conversely, if the Lido precedent is overturned, the entire theoretical framework of DAO governance liability discussed in this article will be significantly impacted. Hierarchy of Recourse: Theory Meets Practice Legal liability is one issue. Actual recourse is another. The most legally convincing defendants (KelpDAO, 60% fault) may be the most difficult to recourse against (offshore DAOs, unknown entity structures). The most accessible defendant in practice (LayerZero Labs Canada Inc., 40% fault) is a real corporation with identifiable directors and over $120 million in venture capital. This creates a tiered system of recourse where practical considerations take precedence over pure liability attribution: Priority 1: LayerZero Labs Canada Inc. A real federal Canadian corporation (company number 13558479, Vancouver),[41] with two directors and ample funds. It is the most viable target for corporate litigation. Key advantages: Identifiable entity, governed by Canadian corporations law, assets can be seized. Key obstacles: Comparison of fault reductions (40% × $292 million = maximum exposure of approximately $117 million), potential arbitration clauses, protection under the Canadian Business Judgment Rule. Priority 2: Audit and security firm. KelpDAO and LayerZero almost certainly engaged a security audit firm to review the bridging contract. According to the Restatement of Torts (Second Edition), professionals who provide information for business guidance are liable for losses caused by negligent misrepresentation. [42] Professional negligence litigation is feasible if any auditor reviews the bridging deployment but fails to flag the 1-of-1 DVN configuration as a significant risk. Audit firms are real entities with errors and omissions insurance, making them one of the most practical targets for recourse. Key threshold question: Was the DVN configuration within the scope of the audit? Obtaining and reviewing the engagement letter is the first step in assessing this claim. Priority 3: KelpDAO founders. Amitej Gajjala and Dheeraj Borra are identifiable individuals who co-founded KelpDAO. They face personal liability as general partners under the Lido/bZx partnership framework and California Corporation Code §16306. The recourse challenge remains if their personal assets are overseas or held in cryptocurrency. Priority 4: Aave Governance Delegates. Identifiable institutional delegates who voted in favor of Proposal 434 (93% LTV). Novel theory, high legal uncertainty, but strong factual basis. Conclusion: A Framework of Shared Responsibility The KelpDAO attack is not a story of a single villain. It is a story of layered failures in a composable system, where each participant—KelpDAO, LayerZero, Aave governance, and the unnamed RPC provider—made decisions that, individually, seemed reasonable but collectively created catastrophic vulnerabilities. KelpDAO chose speed and simplicity over security. LayerZero operated the verification infrastructure without hardening it against known attack vectors. Aave governance set aggressive risk parameters without assessing the underlying bridging security of the collateral. Somewhere in the infrastructure stack, the RPC provider failed to detect or prevent the replacement of its core binary. The 60/40 fault-sharing reflects the judgment that KelpDAO's proactive choice to opt for a 1-of-1 approach for a billion-dollar bridge, based on explicit advice, was more reprehensible. However, LayerZero's 40% share acknowledges that operating the DVN infrastructure carries a separate duty of care that cannot be negated by a disclaimer, especially when the attack vector falls into a known, documented vulnerability category. The terms of service upon which the two agreements rely, setting liability caps of $200 and $50 for the $292 million loss, are rate-limiting signs that only became apparent after the incident. They were drafted to limit risk exposure, but the doctrine of manifest unfairness, the gross negligence exception, and anti-waiver provisions of federal securities laws all provide avenues to invalidate them. The most powerful path, classifying rsETH as a security according to the Howey Test, would not only invalidate the cap clause but also open up private rescission rights and fraud claims that bypass arbitration hurdles. For the broader DeFi ecosystem, this attack establishes several precedents that will shape protocol design and governance for years to come: First, a single point of failure bridging configuration is inherently unreasonable for any protocol protecting significant value. The cost of multiple DVN configurations is negligible relative to the risk. Any protocol that continues to operate a 1-of-1 DVN configuration after this attack is doing so knowingly. Second, infrastructure providers cannot evade responsibility for operational failures through disclaimers. LayerZero's position, "the protocol was not compromised, the configuration was Kelp's choice," is technically accurate but legally inadequate. When you operate validator nodes, choose RPC providers, and sign proofs, the duty of care follows operations, not protocol specifications. Third, DeFi governance is not a shield of liability. The emerging Lido/bZx/Ooki case law is undermining the assumption that DAO governance provides anonymity and protection. Governance voters, especially identifiable institutional delegators, face real personal responsibility for decisions regarding risk parameters. The 93% LTV that turned a $292 million bridging attack into a $200 million bad debt crisis is precisely the type of governance failure that Caremark's oversight obligation aims to capture. The question now is whether the legal system can allocate responsibility in a way that both compensates victims and incentivizes the creation of better security architectures. The answer depends on which recourse paths are pursued, whether assets can be traced quickly, and whether courts are willing to extend traditional liability frameworks to the new architectures of decentralized finance. Implications for protocol developers: In your next bridging deployment, ask yourself a simple question: If this bridge is breached, can I explain in court why I chose the minimum security configuration? If the answer is "because it's cheaper" or "because it's more convenient," you may already be on the side of 60% fault. On the Carroll Towing scale, when prevention costs are negligible while potential losses are in the hundreds of millions, any choice deviating from industry-recommended practices becomes the strongest evidence in the hands of the plaintiff's lawyers.

Implications for governance participants: Anonymous voting no longer equates to anonymous exemption from liability. If you are a trustee of a well-known fund and vote for aggressive LTV parameters without reviewing the underlying risks, your caremark obligations may penetrate the DAO's governance shell and directly affect you personally. The Lido DAO case, regardless of its final outcome, has irreversibly changed the legal landscape of DeFi governance.

Implications for infrastructure providers: "Our protocol has no vulnerabilities" is not a disclaimer. When you operate validation infrastructure, your liability arises with your operational actions, not absolved by your code architecture. The LayerZero case will be a touchstone for testing this principle.

The law is catching up.

An incompatible agreement will find itself on the wrong side of the 60/40 fault-sharing.

data.title: He "printed" $34,000 at Polymarket using a hairdryer.
data.description:
Good Oba, Jinse Finance

In April 2026, the cryptocurrency prediction platform Polymarket staged a series of absurd yet real "money printing" scenes: someone used only a hairdryer to interfere with weather sensors by blowing hot air, manipulating temperature prediction contracts twice and profiting a total of nearly $34,000.

This was neither a sophisticated hacking attack nor a hidden code vulnerability, but rather the most primitive and lowest-cost physical intervention.

... This farcical operation directly exposes the core truth of prediction markets: no matter how sophisticated the smart contract on-chain, it is still vulnerable to physical intervention in the off-chain real world.

How can a hair dryer "print money" in Web3?

At 18:30 on April 6th, the weather station at Charles de Gaulle Airport in France experienced strange temperature fluctuations: the temperature rose sharply by about 4°C within just 12 minutes, briefly reaching 22.5°C before quickly falling back.

In contrast, traditional betting is extremely costly to manipulate: bribing referees requires millions of dollars, bribing athletes requires building long-term networks of relationships, and also incurs severe criminal liability. But in prediction markets, manipulators only need a hairdryer and publicly available sensor location information to easily profit—this temptation of "low investment, high return" is almost irresistible.

... 3. The Inherent Vulnerability of Low-Liquidity Markets According to DeFiLlama data, Hyperliquid's trading volume in the past 24 hours was approximately $7 billion, while the total trading volume of the entire prediction market in the past 30 days was only $20 billion—meaning that Hyperliquid's trading volume in three days is close to the trading volume of the entire prediction market in one month. Low liquidity means that small amounts of capital can significantly influence market prices and even directly manipulate the outcome. In the hairdryer incident, the manipulator did not need to fight against the liquidity of the entire market; he only needed to ensure that the temperature briefly reached the threshold before settlement to lock in profits. Furthermore, prediction markets have an inherent liquidity trap: their contracts are mostly "event-driven," active only within a specific event window. Once the event ends and the contract settles, liquidity instantly drops to zero. The 2024 US presidential election caused Polymarket's monthly trading volume to surge to $1.9 billion, but this peak was unsustainable. The consistently low liquidity made manipulation easier. This is precisely the core reason why Polymarket and Kalshi announced their entry into the perpetual contract market on the same day in April 2026—they must find a way to maintain long-term liquidity; otherwise, manipulation will only escalate. How can prediction markets save themselves? Following the "hair dryer" incident, the Polymarket community and industry insiders proposed various improvement plans to try to address the loopholes in the prediction market mechanism. Here are some mainstream approaches, their respective advantages, disadvantages, and applicable scenarios: 1. Multi-Data Source Verification Solution: Abandoning a single data source, this approach selects multiple geographically dispersed sensors or data interfaces, using their average or median values ​​as the basis for settlement. Simultaneously, an anomaly detection algorithm is added to identify and exclude obviously abnormal data (such as sudden temperature rises or falls in a short period). Advantages: Simple to implement, low development and operation costs; significantly increases the difficulty of manipulation—the manipulator needs to intervene in multiple dispersed data sources simultaneously, greatly increasing costs and risks; this is also the core idea proposed by the Citrini Institute during its research in the Strait of Hormuz, using cross-validation of multi-source data to compensate for the blind spots of a single data source. Disadvantages: Still relies on centralized data sources (such as weather stations, official APIs), failing to fundamentally achieve decentralization; if multiple data sources are geographically close, they may still be manipulated simultaneously; anomaly detection algorithms may be bypassed (e.g., controlling the manipulation amplitude to disguise it as natural fluctuations). Applicable Scenarios: Physical events such as weather and temperature, and scenarios where data sources are easily obtainable and can be verified through multiple channels. 2. Decentralized Oracle Network Solution: Employs mature decentralized oracle networks such as Chainlink and UMA. Multiple independent nodes collect and submit data, filtering outliers through a consensus mechanism. Only data agreed upon by a majority of nodes can be used as the basis for settlement. Nodes that maliciously submit false data will be penalized with the forfeiture of their security deposit. Advantages: Truly achieves decentralization of the data source, eliminating single points of failure; high cost for malicious node actions, effectively curbing the submission of false data; mature solutions are already available for platform integration, eliminating the need for development from scratch. As a leading player in decentralized oracles, Chainlink has long provided data security solutions for the prediction market through an "on-chain + off-chain" collaborative model. However, its governance still faces the "impossible triangle" problem—how to find a balance between data objectivity, processing efficiency, and node security. Disadvantages: High operating costs, as the platform needs to pay service fees to oracle nodes; data submission requires waiting for consensus among multiple nodes, increasing settlement delays; for physical events such as temperature and weather, nodes still rely on external sensors, unable to completely eliminate dependence on physical devices. Applicable scenarios: Financial data (such as cryptocurrency prices, interest rates, and exchange rates), events accessible via APIs, and markets with high decentralization requirements. 3. Delayed Settlement + Community Arbitration Solution: After the contract settlement results are announced, a dispute period (e.g., 24 hours) is set. If community users discover data anomalies, they can initiate a challenge, and the final settlement result is determined by community voting. Simultaneously, a reasonable incentive mechanism is designed to encourage users to report manipulation. Advantages: Introducing human judgment can effectively identify events like "technically sound but logically anomalous" manipulation (like a hair dryer); it increases the likelihood of accountability after manipulation is discovered, reducing the manipulator's sense of impunity. Disadvantages: Settlement delays affect user experience, especially for short-term traders seeking immediate profits; community voting may be manipulated by large investors (refer to the 2025 UMA oracle manipulation incident); a robust challenge incentive mechanism is needed, otherwise users will lack the motivation to report. Applicable Scenarios: High-value contracts, highly controversial events (such as geopolitics, policy changes). 4. Restricting High-Risk Markets Solution: For events easily manipulated by physical/human means (such as weather, single sporting events, individual behavior), set betting limits, increase margin requirements, or directly remove such markets, guiding users to markets less susceptible to manipulation (such as financial data prediction). Advantages: Directly reduces the incentive to manipulate—low betting limits limit the potential profits from manipulation, naturally deterring manipulators; simple to implement, requiring no complex technical modifications. Disadvantages: Restricts market diversity, potentially leading to user churn and pushing some users to unregulated gray-area platforms; cannot fundamentally solve the manipulation problem, only passively mitigates risk. Applicable Scenarios: Weather, single sporting events, individual behaviors, and other easily manipulated events. Where are the boundaries of prediction markets? The hairdryer incident is not the end of prediction markets, but a necessary growing pain in the industry's rapid development. Decentralization can resist code attacks and prevent hacking, but it cannot withstand physical intervention, let alone curb the profit-seeking nature of capital. For prediction markets to truly mature, they must first recognize their own boundaries: standardized scenarios such as financial data are suitable for prediction, while events involving physical intervention have inherent flaws. Only by acknowledging the loopholes in the mechanism and improving risk control can prediction markets move from unregulated growth to stable compliance and achieve their original goal of aggregating collective wisdom. The manipulator of the hair dryer may never be found, but the lessons learned are worth remembering for the entire industry: at the boundary between on-chain and off-chain, security depends not on the smartest contracts, but on the most vulnerable sensors; not on the most sophisticated code, but on the most rigorous mechanism design. This is not only a challenge for prediction markets, but also a common dilemma for the entire Web3 industry: when decentralization meets the real world, when code logic collides with physical rules, a truly secure and fair system may not need more complex code, but rather humility and reverence for reality, rules, and fairness.

data.title: After AAVE was hit by a scandal, Spark defied the trend and raised $1.3 billion. What did it do right?
data.description: When Aave faced a massive $10 billion exodus, Spark caught the massive liquidity drain. The on-chain disaster triggered by cross-chain vulnerabilities in Kelp DAO and LayzerZero tore the DeFi lending market into two distinct worlds. The influx of the "toxic" asset rsETH into Aave resulted in approximately $200 million in bad debt, causing a liquidity crunch across the network and a massive exodus of billions of dollars. However, amidst this panic, another lending protocol giant, Spark, enjoyed its moment in the spotlight. Its TVL (Total Value Locked) surged by $1.3 billion, and ETH deposit rates soared to 130%, making it the preferred safe haven for whales transferring assets. This black swan event re-evaluated who would claim the DeFi throne. Aave experienced a massive run, with Spark taking advantage to siphon $1.3 billion. The collapse of rsETH's cross-chain bridge brought the entire Aave lending market to a standstill. Hackers used illegally minted rsETH as collateral to borrow large amounts of WETH from Aave, emptying the clean assets and leaving behind a pool of bad debts. Panic spread rapidly like a virus: in just three and a half days, $15.1 billion fled Aave, reducing total deposits from $48.5 billion to $30.7 billion, with about one-third of the funds fleeing; WETH utilization on multiple chains reached 100%; depositors were unable to withdraw funds, and liquidators had no money to lend. The most eye-catching move came from Justin Sun, who quickly withdrew 65,584 ETH from Aave, worth approximately $154 million. This "first-come, first-served" behavior created a herd effect in the market. For investors, even the highest annualized returns couldn't offset the panic of not being able to withdraw their principal. While Aave became a liquidity outlet for hackers, Spark became an escape route for users. Spark's TVL increased by $1.3 billion, reaching a total of $4.74 billion. This money was a vote of confidence from the market, delivered in real cash. Due to a surge in lending demand into Spark, coupled with a severe shortage of liquidity, the ETH deposit rate on Spark experienced a spectacular spike, reaching an annualized rate of 130%, directly reflecting the extremely high premium of safe-haven assets. Spark's ability to absorb this demand is attributed to its unique ecosystem structure. Unlike Aave, it serves as the lending engine of the Sky ecosystem, backed by a massive USDS reserve. As Sky's liquidity outpost, Spark not only relies on external deposits but also receives stablecoin replenishment directly through Sky's credit lines. This "central bank-level" liquidity backing ensures that withdrawal channels remain open even during market turmoil. Spark, abandoning the vanity of TVL (Liquidity Re-collateralized Tokens), delisted rsETH against the trend. Spark avoided the rsETH debacle thanks to a contrarian decision made three months ago. Different fates on the same day. On January 29th, the two major lending platforms diverged in their logic regarding the handling of Liquidity Re-collateralized Tokens (LRT). Aave went all out. The protocol officially launched rsETH E-Mode, allowing users to leverage lending with a high collateralization ratio (LTV) of 93%. Aave's goal was to restore WETH utilization and boost TVL and revenue by attracting the expected $1 billion in rsETH inflows. Spark made a cautious retreat. The protocol completely stopped new rsETH supply through the governance operation Spell and gradually removed it from the asset list. This move by Spark caused strong dissatisfaction among ETH revolving leverage users, who often used repeated collateralization of assets like stETH or rsETH to profit from interest rate spreads. Spark's delisting forced them to migrate their positions, with most flowing to Aave, which offered more lenient policies and lower interest rates. At the time, the community questioned Spark's team, arguing they were "too conservative" or "abandoning growth." No one expected that this step would later save the entire protocol. In a post-mortem analysis, Spark's Strategy Director, monetsupply.eth, pointed out that the decision to delist rsETH was based on a security-oriented tightening mechanism. Marginal Cost vs. Marginal Benefit: If the cost of maintaining an asset exceeds its risk-adjusted return to the protocol, that asset will be liquidated. Risk Exposure Concentration: rsETH had extremely low utilization on Spark, almost monopolized by a single wallet address, making risk difficult to diversify. User Preference Survey: rsETH's only whale user expressed a willingness to actively migrate to more mature collateral such as wstETH or weETH, providing the protocol with an opportunity to smoothly liquidate assets. It is precisely this transparency and discipline in decision-making—"not blindly pursuing TVL"—that allowed Spark to avoid all potential losses that could have resulted from hackers exploiting rsETH. A multi-layered risk control system: rate limiting + interest rate buffer + isolation architecture. PANews believes that even without delisting rsETH, Spark's architecture is sufficient to withstand such risks. Compared to Aave's pursuit of capital efficiency at the expense of security redundancy, Spark has established a multi-layered, deep defense system. Spark implements strict rate-limited caps, meaning that the amount of funds deposited and borrowed increases sequentially over a fixed period. Even if rsETH were not delisted, attackers would not be able to deposit $290 million in collateral at once as they could on Aave. This design forcibly limits the maximum risk exposure of a single event, rigidly suppressing losses within an acceptable range. Spark maintains a relatively high interest rate ceiling in the long term. Under stable market conditions, higher borrowing rates, while discouraging excessive borrowing (if the cost is too high, people won't borrow), also attract more people to deposit money (those who deposit money earn more). As a result, the pool always maintains liquidity, preventing a "runaway" situation where everyone wants to withdraw their funds. Especially during market crashes, there is no run on the bank due to liquidity shortages. When the pool utilization rate increases, Spark's interest rate curve will be steeper than Aave's, which will have two significant consequences: Forced deleveraging: High interest costs will force borrowers to actively seek liquidity to repay loans. Attracting Supplemental Liquidity: High annualized deposit yields will quickly attract external arbitrage capital, thus resolving the 100% utilization bottleneck. Spark's modular, isolated architecture provides strong control over risk management. When dealing with high-risk synthetic assets like USDe, Spark adopts a prudent approach, isolating them in specific primary risk pools to ensure that even if a particular asset experiences problems, it won't affect the platform's main lending pools. The massive migration of liquidity from Aave to Spark signifies a shift in capital preferences from pursuing returns to prioritizing safety and stability. Aave's multi-billion dollar outflow serves as a wake-up call for all protocols that pursue high capital efficiency. When safety margins are sacrificed, even minor externally related risks can escalate into a global crisis for the protocol.

data.title: Kevin Walsh, worth $200 million, is about to face three major challenges.
data.description:
2026Year leaf="">·His current position includesDeFiLending AgreementCompound, derivatives platformdYdXandLighter, and

But the transformation will begin in 2025. In May 2025, he stated in a public interview: "We are at the forefront of AI use cases, and everything that technology touches will become cheaper." In November, in his Wall Street Journal column, he directly defined AI as a significant deflationary force that could boost productivity and enhance American competitiveness.

2025End of 2025 to2026In multiple podcasts and interviews, he repeatedly emphasized that AIis“

Democratic Senator Elizabeth Warren·Warren used this description at the hearing to attack him, arguing that he was pandering to Trump."Position reversal"This description was used to attack him, suggesting that he was pandering to Trump.

Walsh responded by citing Greenspan's case from the 1990s: From 1995 to 2000, the average annual growth rate of US non-farm labor productivity was... The average hourly output growth rate in the non-financial corporate sector reached 3.5%, almost double the 1.4% growth rate of the previous eight years. At the time, the labor market was extremely tight, with the unemployment rate at its lowest level in decades, but core inflation remained stable below 2%, failing to rise in tandem with economic growth. Greenspan chose not to rush into tightening policy, ultimately achieving a balance between economic growth and price stability. Walsh believes he is making the same judgment now, that AI is this round of the internet. However, this judgment is facing severe pressure from reality. 2026Year3Month's CPIyear-on-year rose to3.3%, higher than2month2.4%, yes2024 leaf="">CPIRising to 2.6%year-on-year. The situation in Iran has driven up energy prices, with gasoline prices rising 18.9% month-on-month and fuel oil prices rising 44.2%, directly contributing to the largest monthly increase in overall inflation since June 2022. He acknowledged in the hearing that there is still work to be done on current inflation data, while refusing to provide any specific interest rate path or timetable. At the start of the hearing, Warren used the term "puppet" in her opening statement, citing Trump's social media post from the previous week that "Kevin will lower interest rates after he takes office." He then repeatedly pressed the president: Have you committed to a specific interest rate path? If inflation rises again, can you withstand pressure from the White House to cut interest rates? Warsh's answer was that the president has never asked him to pre-set, commit to, or fix any interest rate decisions in any conversation, nor would he make such a commitment. He stated that independence is not a firewall automatically granted by law, but something the Federal Reserve has earned itself by upholding price stability and avoiding overstepping its bounds. If the Federal Reserve continues to make mistakes and overstep its bounds, public and political scrutiny will be a reasonable price to pay, and its independence will be eroded from within; political pressure is merely an external factor. The inflation in 2021 to 2022, in his assessment, was not simply a misjudgment, but rather a result of the Federal Reserve using its credibility to endorse fiscal expansion and actively blurring the boundaries between monetary and fiscal policies. This is the real crisis of independence he speaks of, not caused by Trump, but by the Federal Reserve itself.

This set of logic has taken shape as early as2010. That year, he gave a speech titled "An Ode to Independence," which subsequently appeared repeatedly in interviews at the Hoover Institution and in columns in the Wall Street Journal. The core of his argument was the same: the biggest threat to the Federal Reserve did not come from external political pressure, but from its own gradual ceding of institutional space. The test of independence comes not only from Trump himself. Republican Senator Thom Tillis announced at the meeting that he would temporarily suspend his support for Warsh's confirmation. The reason isn't questioning Warsh himself, but rather the Justice Department's ongoing criminal investigation into current Chairman Powell, ostensibly concerning overspending on the Federal Reserve headquarters renovations. Powell and a federal judge both believe this is political pressure regarding monetary policy. Tillis's position is that proceeding with confirmation under this shadow has tainted the entire process with politics. This means Warsh's confirmation timeline is stalled, regardless of his answers during the hearing. Can balance sheet reduction and interest rate cuts proceed simultaneously? Warsh's views on the balance sheet, formed when he left the Federal Reserve in 2011, are his most stable views over the past fifteen years. He used the word "bloated" to describe the Federal Reserve's current balance sheet, which is approximately $6.7 trillion. QE, initially a temporary emergency measure during the 2008 financial crisis, became a semi-permanent tool for over a decade. This evolution led to two structural consequences: the boundaries between monetary and fiscal policy became blurred, with the Federal Reserve effectively assuming some fiscal functions; and large-scale asset purchases systematically inflated financial asset prices, benefiting those who held stocks and real estate, while ordinary households did not receive the same benefits. Therefore, this balance sheet must be significantly reduced, while emphasizing that any reduction must be cautious, orderly, and fully communicated to avoid causing unnecessary shocks to the market. This is a combination that makes the market uneasy: he may simultaneously push forward with balance sheet reduction and interest rate cuts, withdrawing liquidity from the balance sheet while simultaneously sending easing signals through interest rates, with both directions affecting market pricing. His explanation was that interest rates should once again become the primary tool of monetary policy, while asset purchases should revert to their temporary role from the crisis period—reclaiming misused tools and allowing the right tools to function properly again. Following the hearing, US Treasury yields rose, with the market pricing in the uncertainty of this mixed scenario through actual trading. He also mentioned another specific reform during the meeting: to launch a data project tracking billions of real-time prices to replace the portion of the existing CPI statistical framework that relies on lagged sampling. This would reduce the frequency with which officials publicly predict interest rate paths, because once a prediction is made, officials often stick to it to maintain credibility even after circumstances change, which is the source of slow response. He called the state he wanted FOMC a healthy internal debate, rather than following a pre-written script to reach a predetermined conclusion. He summarized this direction as a "system transition," a complete shift in policy and system, not just changing one or two parameters. He also mentioned that stablecoins and on-chain price data could become more real-time supplementary indicators to compensate for the shortcomings of existing statistical frameworks.
