This also reveals a deeper logic in his view of crypto: it's not just an asset class that needs to be regulated, but also an information infrastructure that can be used to improve the quality of policy judgments. His $130 million holding may also be understood from this perspective.

data.title: GPT vs Gemini vs Seedream: Who will reign supreme?
data.description:
Author: Denise, Biteye Content Team

In April 2026, the AI-generated image field officially entered a "three-way competition" phase.

On April 21st, OpenAI suddenly released GPT-Image-2, directly consigning the DALL·E series to history; not long ago, Google upgraded Gemini image generation to Gemini 3.1 Flash Image (i.e., Nano Banana 2), achieving Pro-level image quality at Flash speeds; domestically, ByteDance's Seed team's Seedream continues to iterate, firmly holding the top choice for creators.

The three companies are taking completely different paths—OpenAI pursues ultimate semantic understanding, Google bets on speed and multimodal editing, and ByteDance bets on aesthetics and localization. Who is the true king? Let's break it down one by one.

I. Core Positioning: Who exactly are they?

GPT-Image-2 (OpenAI)

Tag: Logic Master

Core Advantages: Extremely strong semantic understanding; even if you write a short essay in the prompt, it can accurately break down every detail and logical relationship. Its text rendering capability is near pixel-perfect, making it the top choice for posters, UI, and product images.

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image (Google)

Tag: All-Round Speed ​​King

Core Advantages: Speed, realism, and natural language editing capabilities all combined.

Offering near-Nano Banana Pro image quality, world knowledge, and command compliance capabilities at Flash speed settings, providing the smoothest mobile experience and extremely convenient multimodal editing.

Seedream 5.0 Lite (ByteDance)

Tags: Art + Cost-Effective Pioneer

Core Advantages: Top-tier global illumination, artistic composition, and character consistency, especially with a clear local advantage in Chinese contexts, Eastern aesthetics, and scenes blending ancient and modern styles. Most user-friendly for domestic access, lowest cost.

...

II. Quick Start Guide

III. Practical Testing in Four Core Dimensions

Referring to GenAI-Bench and DrawBench, the editor selected 4 sets of the most representative prompts, generating 5 images for each of the three models in each set, and using the best image for subjective comparison.

The following are the test results + key prompts:

Dimension A: Semantic Compliance

Test prompt: "A rabbit wearing a white spacesuit eats steaming hot xiaolongbao on the neon-lit Bund in Shanghai. Behind it is a reflective glass curtain wall in the rainy night, reflecting a cyberpunk scene of flying cars in 2050. Cinematic lighting, surreal details, 8K quality."

Test results:

GPT-Image-2:

Dimension B: Image Quality and Artistic Style

Test prompt (product photography + portrait): "Close-up of Apple Vision Pro packaging box, mirrored metal reflection, brand text clearly visible, professional studio lighting, studio environment, ultimate realism."

Actual test results:

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image:

Gemini 3.1 Flash Image: Strongest in realism and commercial usability.

... It features a classic white packaging design, with the glasses naturally partially visible inside the box. Accessories and instructions are logically arranged alongside, creating a complete and professional composition. The brand name is clearly visible, and the lighting is soft and natural. The textures of different materials—cardboard, metal, and glass—are remarkably close to those captured by a real camera, giving it the feel of an "official product promotional image," leading the pack in terms of realism. Seedream 5.0 Lite: Seedream 5.0 Lite: Its most stunning aspects are the subtlety of its lighting and the artistic atmosphere. It adopts a minimalist, high-end close-up angle, focusing entirely on the Vision Pro packaging box. The silver Apple logo and the metallic "Vision Pro" lettering have an extremely realistic and delicate embossed texture and high-gloss reflections. The white box's material rendering and soft shadow transitions are natural and smooth, giving the overall product a high-end photographic feel, exuding sophistication and elegance.

GPT-Image-2

GPT-Image-2: The material rendering and lighting effects are the most advanced. It treats the packaging box with a cool silver metallic texture, with strong and layered high-gloss reflections. The glasses are visible through the box window, and the transition between the metal surface and the glass lenses is extremely delicate. The overall image is high-end and futuristic, perfectly reproducing the dramatic lighting of a professional photography studio, showcasing a strong "product advertising-grade" quality. Summary: Gemini 3.1 Flash Image excels in realism and commercial appeal in product photography; GPT-Image-2 stands out for its metallic texture rendering and advanced lighting; Seedream 5.0 Lite wins with its delicate lighting and artistic quality. All three achieve top-tier image quality, but with different focuses.

Dimension C: Chinese and English Comprehension and Cultural Context

Test prompt: "The imagery of Li Bai's 'Quiet Night Thoughts': 'Before my bed, the bright moonlight shines, I wonder if it is frost on the ground.' A woman in ancient style looks up at the moon in a Tang Dynasty courtyard. The moonlight shines on the blue bricks and white walls, and the ink painting style blends naturally with the real light and shadow, creating a cinematic atmosphere."

Test Results:

GPT-Image-2

GPT-Image-2: Excellent performance.

... It accurately recreates the classic imagery of "Moonlight before my bed, I suspect it is frost on the ground," depicting a woman gracefully and serenely gazing at the moon. The moonlight casts a large area of ​​light on the blue bricks and white walls, creating a clear contrast of light and shadow. Elements such as the classical courtyard, tiled eaves, and bamboo shadows are complete and layered, resulting in a very prominent cinematic quality in the overall lighting and shadow effects. However, the poetic fusion of ink-wash aesthetics is relatively restrained, leaning more towards a realistic cinematic style. Seedream 5.0 Lite: Excellent. The fusion of ink-wash aesthetics and realistic lighting is natural and outstanding. A woman in ancient Chinese style gazes at the moon in a Tang Dynasty courtyard. Moonlight spills across the blue bricks and white walls, creating a clear effect of "frost on the ground," successfully recreating the serene poetic atmosphere of "Quiet Night Thoughts." The classical ambiance and cinematic lighting are delicate and elegant, exuding a rich cultural charm. (Gemini 3.1 Flash Image) The atmosphere is very strong. The woman stands on the courtyard corridor, gazing at the moon. Her classical clothing is richly colored, and the lanterns, artificial hills, trees, and distant night scenery are well-arranged. The interplay of moonlight and night creates a strong cinematic visual effect, offering excellent immersion. However, it falls slightly short in conveying the traditional ink painting style and the ethereal poetic sentiment unique to "Quiet Night Thoughts," approaching more of a conventional high-quality ancient-style night scene. Summary: In terms of understanding the Chinese cultural context and the poetic imagery of "Quiet Night Thoughts," Seedream 5.0 Lite demonstrates a clear local advantage and artistic warmth; GPT-Image-2 excels in cinematic realistic lighting; Gemini 3.1 Flash Image has a balanced overall atmosphere, but its Eastern classical charm is slightly weaker. Dimension D: Generation Speed ​​and Interactive Experience Based on the overall experience throughout the testing process, Gemini 3.1 Flash Image leads in speed and mobile experience; Seedream 5.0 Lite is the smoothest in domestic access and handling long Chinese prompts; GPT-Image-2 wins with its conversational and precise image editing in thinking mode. IV. Watermarking and Compliance Considerations Global regulation of AI-generated images is rapidly tightening in 2026. For creators needing commercial use, brand collaborations, copyright protection, or platform distribution, watermarking and metadata standards have become crucial decision-making points. Gemini 3.1 Flash Image: Employs a dual-layer authentication system of SynthID invisible pixel-level watermark + C2PA metadata credentials, with a visible sparkle icon in the lower right corner of the image. GPT-Image-2: Continuing OpenAI's C2PA content credential system, it embeds signature source information at the file metadata layer. Seedream 5.0 Lite: Typically uses platform-level content tagging or basic watermarking mechanisms. The specific implementation varies depending on the product type, leaning more towards application-layer compliance labeling rather than a unified international standard system. Tip: If you primarily work on cross-border commercial projects or require strict copyright protection, GPT-Image-2's C2PA support will be more advantageous; for daily rapid creation, Gemini's SynthID + C2PA dual-layer mechanism is sufficient and comes with a visible identifier for easy traceability. V. Interesting Case Studies of GPT-Image-2 Testing Having covered the serious technical and compliance aspects, we've also selected some interesting real-world testing cases of GPT-Image-2 to give you a more intuitive feel for its potential in "creativity + semantic understanding". After all, the charm of raw image models lies not only in their parameters and benchmark scores, but also in their ability to accurately capture your wildest ideas.

1. The actress from "Girl with a Pearl Earring" is currently using the latest Apple Vision Pro to livestream and sell products.

...

2. Hong Kong Travel Guide: 4 Days 3 Nights

3. Trump's Social Media Posts on His First Day in Office

4. Full Series Product Images of iPhone 18

This is hilarious: Will the iPhone 18 have a foldable screen?

...

5. Generate an image of a Binance account with a large balance

Risk Warning: All images are AI-generated fictional content, used only for demonstrating model capabilities, and do not represent real people or real account status

In Conclusion

data.title: KelpDAO: Cascading Risks and Emergency Response in a Crisis
data.description:
Key points: The $290 million bridging vulnerability in KelpDAO triggered a chain reaction, freezing more than $6.7 billion of WETH liquidity across five chains and affecting users who had never used rsETH. This incident also revealed the real boundaries of the "permissionless" system: the Arbitrum Security Council performed a forced state transition through an atomic contract upgrade authorized by governance, transferring 30,766 ETH without the holder's signature.

On April 18, 2026, KelpDAO's rsETH cross-chain bridge was attacked, resulting in a loss of approximately $290 million, becoming the largest DeFi security incident this year. Initial tracing pointed to the Lazarus Group, a documented state-sponsored attack group that has long targeted crypto infrastructure [1]. This attack did not exploit a smart contract vulnerability, but rather poisoned the RPC infrastructure on which a single decentralized validator network (DVN) node relied, forging cross-chain messages and releasing rsETH tokens without any corresponding destruction on the source chain. LayerZero [1] and KelpDAO [2] have provided detailed descriptions of the attack itself. This article takes a different approach: instead of recounting the attack process, it examines what happened after the attack: how a single point of infrastructure dependency triggered a cascade that froze billions of dollars in liquidity across five chains, and how this cascade forced decentralized governance frameworks to exercise centralized emergency powers in the public eye. The causal chain of the KelpDAO incident spans three layers of the "decentralized" technology stack: the single-point DVN dependency made the attack possible; the composability of DeFi (i.e., "DeFi Lego," the characteristic of protocols interlocking like building blocks) subsequently escalated this bridging vulnerability into a systemic liquidity crisis; and the scale of the crisis, in turn, forced the governance framework to expose the centralized emergency powers embedded within it. Background: A Brief Overview of the KelpDAO Attack KelpDAO is the issuer of rsETH. rsETH is a Liquidity Restaking Token (LRT), representing ETH staked positions distributed across multiple carriers. To enable cross-chain circulation of rsETH, KelpDAO integrated LayerZero's messaging protocol. This protocol relies on DVN (Decentralized Verification Network) to verify the legitimacy of cross-chain messages before execution on the target chain. Key configuration choice: KelpDAO’s rsETH OApp uses a 1-of-1 DVN configuration, with only the LayerZero Labs-operated DVN as the sole validator. This means that the entire cross-chain security of rsETH depends on a single validator entity. LayerZero’s integration documentation explicitly recommends the use of a multi-DVN configuration with redundancy, and LayerZero stated that it had communicated this best practice to KelpDAO before the incident [1]. KelpDAO responded that the 1/1 configuration is “documented in the LayerZero documentation and published as the default configuration for any new OFT deployment” and “has been explicitly identified as the appropriate configuration during L2 scaling” [2]. The attacker compromised two RPC nodes used by the LayerZero Labs DVN and replaced their binaries with malicious versions. These malicious nodes only returned forged on-chain state data to DVN's IP address, behaving normally to all other observers, including LayerZero's own monitoring infrastructure. Meanwhile, a DDoS attack targeting uncompromised RPC nodes forced a failover to the poisoned nodes. Result: DVN confirmed a cross-chain message that never occurred on the source chain, releasing 116,500 rsETH from the Ethereum-side adapter (0x85d4...8ef3) [1, 3] without any corresponding source chain destruction. The release transaction was 0x1ae232...db4222. On-chain evidence was clear: the Ethereum target endpoint accepted nonce 308, while the maximum outbound nonce reported by the Unichain source endpoint remained 307 [10]. KelpDAO detected the anomaly within 46 minutes and suspended all related contracts. This prevented a follow-up attack on an additional 40,000 rsETH (approximately $95 million) [2]. But by then, the attackers had moved on to the next phase: converting the stolen rsETH into lending assets through DeFi lending protocols. From counterfeit tokens to lending assets The attackers did not directly sell the stolen rsETH. The 116,500 tokens were distributed across seven fork wallets and monetized through various channels, including direct exchange for ETH via aggregators, Compound V3 supply positions, and bridging to Arbitrum [10]. But the most far-reaching path went through Aave: the attackers deposited 89,567 rsETH (approximately $221 million) into two Aave lending markets on two chains: Ethereum Core and Arbitrum. Using Aave's E-Mode (a capital efficiency feature that allows related assets to achieve a higher loan-to-value ratio), the attacker borrowed 82,620 WETH and 821 wstETH as collateral for deposited rsETH [3]. These positions were leveraged to the limit. The health factors of the attacker's seven addresses ranged from 1.01 to 1.03, just slightly above the liquidation threshold [3]. This was possible because Aave's E-Mode set a 93% LTV for rsETH, a 95% liquidation threshold, and a safety buffer of only 2 percentage points. The details of each address in the two markets are as follows: Table 1: Attacker's rsETH supply and WETH/wstETH lending details in the two Aave markets. Data source: On-chain data statistics, compiled from Etherscan, Arbiscan, and DeBank, as of 16:51 UTC on 2026-04-22. USD value reflects the token price at the time of each transaction.

Cascade Effect: How a Bridge Vulnerability Frozen WETH on Five Chains

The diagram below outlines the complete cascade chain. Steps 1 and 2 (the bridge vulnerability and Aave collateral deposit) have already been described in the background section above. This section delves into steps 3 through 5: why WETH had to be frozen, which parameters shaped the severity of the cascade, and the actual cost of the freeze.

Cascade Effect: How a Bridge Vulnerability Frozen WETH on Five Chains

Figure 1: Cascade process from bridging vulnerability to WETH freeze on five chains

Why WETH had to be frozen

On April 19, Aave's Protocol Guardian froze all rsETH and wrsETH markets on Aave V3 and V4, prohibiting new deposits and loans using rsETH as collateral [8]. This was the expected first step in the response. The unexpected second step occurred on April 20: Aave froze the WETH reserves on Ethereum, Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea [3, 8]. Why freeze WETH? This is an unattacked asset that has nothing to do with cross-chain bridges. Because the attacker deposited rsETH, which was minted without any corresponding source chain assets. Aave's oracles continued to price these tokens at full market prices, treating them as valid collateral indistinguishable from legitimately bridged rsETH. The attacker exploited this information asymmetry to borrow real WETH as collateral representing unsecured liabilities at the system level. This drained the WETH from the lending pools, bringing the utilization of the affected markets to 100%. With full utilization, existing WETH depositors could not withdraw their funds, and liquidators could not obtain the underlying assets required to perform liquidation. The liquidation mechanism, the core line of defense against bad debts in the protocol, was effectively paralyzed [3]. If WETH lending remains open, the remaining liquidity in other on-chain pools can be drained through the same mechanism: deposit rsETH, borrow WETH, and leave. Freezing WETH is not an option, but the only way to control the damage. The three parameters shaping the cascade are not accidental. Three protocol parameters determine the scale of the direct damage and the resulting ripple effect of the freeze. 1. LTV: How much healthy assets can be withdrawn per unit of contaminated collateral. Aave's E-Mode sets the LTV for rsETH at 93%, meaning that for every $1 of contaminated rsETH deposited, $0.93 of WETH can be borrowed. In comparison, Spark Protocol's rsETH LTV was 72% and Fluid's was about 75% during the same period [3]. Aave's parameters are the most aggressive in the market. This is a well-thought-out design decision, not an oversight. In January 2026, Aave governance increased rsETH's E-Mode LTV from 92.5% to 93%, further compressing the already thin safety buffer from 2.5% to 2% [3]. The base (non-E-Mode) LTV was deliberately set close to zero (0.05%), effectively forcing all meaningful rsETH borrowing through the high LTV E-Mode path. 2. Pool Depth: Market Vulnerability to Liquidity Withdrawal The same amount of borrowing has drastically different effects in target pools at different depths.

Table 2: WETH Reserve Size and Direct Attacker Withdrawal Ratio in Aave V3 Markets for Each Chain

The attackers only deposited rsETH in the Aave V3 market. Aave V4 (deployed only on Ethereum, launched on 2026-03-30) was also subject to preventative rsETH freezing [8], but this is not reflected in this table. WETH reserve data comes from LlamaRisk [3]; attacker lending data comes from the address details table above.

The attackers focused their borrowing operations on Ethereum Core and Arbitrum. But the key observation is what happened on those chains that the attackers never touched. Since rsETH is accepted as collateral on Mantle, Base, and Linea, any existing user positions on these chains that are collateralized with rsETH are at risk of bad debt should the underlying bridging support be breached. Aave’s decision to preemptively freeze WETH on all five chains is a reasonable response: if these markets were left open, they would be exposed to the same extraction mechanism that the attacker has already verified on Ethereum and Arbitrum [3, 8].

3. Number of Cross-Chain Deployments: Scope of Freeze Spread

rsETH is listed as collateral on 11 of the 23 Aave V3 markets, with substantial exposure on 7 of them [3]. The attacker operated on only 2 chains, but the preemptive WETH freeze affected at least 5 chains, including markets where the attacker never deposited a single token. LTV determines how much is extracted on each chain, and pool depth determines the degree of impact on each market. But it was the number of chains that used rsETH as collateral that ultimately determined the extent of the freeze. These parameters were not static. Nine days before the attack, on April 9, Aave’s Risk Steward increased the supply cap of rsETH: Ethereum Core from 480,000 to 530,000 and Mantle from 52,000 to 70,000 [3]. While this does not imply causation (the attacker’s preparation period was likely earlier than these adjustments), it highlights how routine parameter adjustments can inadvertently amplify the impact of future events. The Real Impact of the Freeze: A $290 million bridging vulnerability resulted in the freezing of WETH liquidity on five chains, affecting combined reserves of over $6.7 billion in the affected markets. Direct losses were limited to the amount borrowed by the attacker. But in DeFi lending, a freeze is far from a minor operational disruption. It locks up user liquidity, prevents withdrawals, disrupts active positions, and weakens the liquidation mechanisms that protect protocols from bad debts. The vast majority of affected users have never been exposed to rsETH, KelpDAO, or any cross-chain bridge. They are WETH depositors and borrowers on Aave, participating in what they reasonably believe to be a straightforward lending market. WETH is the most basic liquid asset in DeFi. Freezing it is equivalent to shutting down the withdrawal channel of the largest bank in the city because another financial institution was scammed using a product that most depositors have never heard of. LlamaRisk’s event report [3] established two bad debt scenario models and gave chain-by-chain shortfall predictions, which is currently the most detailed risk propagation analysis. But even this analysis focuses on potential bad debts rather than the broader operational costs brought about by the freeze itself, including withdrawal lock-up, position disruption, and weakened liquidation capabilities in all affected markets. A full quantification of the overall impact of the cascade remains an open question. If the attack cascade is complex, the recovery process is equally complex. Composability constrains both the destruction and the repair. Aave cannot simply "unfreeze everything." Each market needs to be assessed independently, taking into account local rsETH exposure, WETH utilization, and attacker activity, and faces different risk profiles. The timeline clearly illustrates this: April 19: Protocol Guardian froze all rsETH and wrsETH reserves on Aave V3 and V4 [8]. April 20: WETH was frozen on Ethereum, Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea [3, 8]. April 21: Only WETH on Ethereum Core V3 was unfrozen, with LTV remaining at 0 as a precaution. WETH on Ethereum Prime, Arbitrum, Base, Mantle, and Linea remained frozen [8]. Four days after the attack, only one of the six affected markets was unfrozen. The recovery path was as complex as the attack path: protocol-by-protocol, chain-by-chain, each step requiring governance coordination and risk assessment. Emergency Response: How Arbitrum Transferred 30,766 ETH Without Holder Signatures While Aave was handling the lending cascade, a parallel response was underway on Arbitrum. On April 21, the Arbitrum Security Council announced an emergency action to freeze 30,766 ETH held by the attacker on Arbitrum One.[6] These funds were transferred to an intermediate freeze address (0x...0DA0) and can only be disposed of through a subsequent Arbitrum governance vote.[7] The Arbitrum Security Council is a formal part of the Arbitrum DAO governance structure and is not an external entity or ad hoc committee. The emergency action was publicly announced through the Arbitrum governance forum.[7] It was carried out after law enforcement confirmed the attacker's identity.[6] The full transaction details were provided for anyone to verify. The Security Council acted within its established mandate, weighing "the commitment to the security and integrity of the Arbitrum community without affecting any Arbitrum users or applications."[6] This was not a closed-door decision, but an emergency action authorized by governance, executed openly and transparently, with clear on-chain evidence. The technical mechanism is what makes this action noteworthy, not the governance decision itself, but its on-chain execution method. Based on BlockSec's Phalcon trace analysis [9], the Security Council adopted an atomic three-step operation: The Upgrade Executor temporarily upgraded the Ethereum inbox contract (DelayedInbox) and added a new function called sendUnsignedTransactionOverride. This function was used to create a cross-chain message impersonating an attacker's address. The message was injected through Bridge.enqueueDelayedMessage, with kind=3 corresponding to L1MessageType_L2Message in the Arbitrum Nitro stack. This message type allows L2MessageKind_UnsignedUserTx to be executed on L2. The key point is that this path does not require signature verification. The sender parameter is switched from the standard msg.sender path to caller-controlled input, carrying the attacker's address context through L1→L2 address aliasing. After the transfer is completed on L2, the inbox contract is restored to its original implementation. The L1 transaction [4] and the resulting L2 transaction [5] are both publicly viewable on Phalcon Explorer. The L2 transaction is displayed as "from attacker to 0x...0DA0", but this is not a standard user-signed transfer, but a chain-level forced state transition: the asset is transferred without the asset owner's private key by escalating permissions through the governance infrastructure. The dilemma of decentralization is straightforward: upgradeable contracts grant unlimited power. If a contract can be upgraded, its behavior can be modified to do anything, including transferring assets without the holder's signature. This is an inherent capability of any system built on upgradeable contracts. 30,766 ETH is currently held in a frozen address. Only a subsequent Arbitrum governance vote will determine its fate. The atomic upgrade-execution-recovery model left no permanent changes to the inbox contract and did not affect any other users or applications [6]. From most reasonable assessments, the actions of the Arbitrum Security Council were correct. The attackers were identified as state actors, law enforcement was involved, the governance process was transparent, $71 million of stolen assets were recovered, or at least further money laundering was prevented. But the capability that makes all this possible extends far beyond this specific case. The same upgrade-execute-recovery mechanism, in principle, can be used to transfer any asset held by any address on Arbitrum One. The Security Council's power is not limited to attacker addresses or stolen funds; it's a general capability governed by governance rules, not just code. This is the dilemma. Users interact with L2 with an implicit mental model: "My assets are controlled by my private key, and no one can transfer them without my signature." The emergency response to the KelpDAO incident demonstrated that this model is incomplete. On Arbitrum, and any L2 with an upgradeable bridging contract and a Security Council, assets can be transferred through governance-level actions that completely bypass signature verification. Arbitrum is not an isolated case. Aave's market freeze was also a governance-driven emergency action. In the KelpDAO incident, multiple protocols simultaneously exercised centralized emergency powers: Aave froze markets on five chains, the Arbitrum Security Council executed forced transfers, and KelpDAO globally suspended contracts. This crisis response within a "decentralized" ecosystem was, in practice, a coordinated exercise of centralized power. The issue isn't whether emergency powers should exist. The KelpDAO case provides strong evidence for their necessity. The issue lies in whether the boundaries, triggering conditions, and accountability mechanisms of these powers are sufficiently transparent. Users who deposit assets on L2 should be able to answer a fundamental question: Under what circumstances can the Security Council transfer my funds? What recourse do I have?

Status of Stolen Funds

Independent on-chain tracking of the stolen funds (see MetaSleuth [11] for full visualization) shows that the attacker dispersed 116,500 rsETH into 7 primary addresses, most of which were deposited into Aave (Ethereum Core and Arbitrum) as collateral to borrow WETH and wstETH. The borrowed tokens were exchanged through a small amount of DEX and then aggregated into the same address 0x5d39...7ccc (Ethereum / Arbitrum) on both chains. As of 05:42 UTC on April 22, 2026, the stolen funds were distributed across four states:

Table 3: Distribution of Stolen Funds in Four States (as of 05:42 UTC on April 22, 2026)

Approximately 31% were frozen or intercepted, 23% remained in an untouched Ethereum address, and 46% had been or was being distributed to 103 primary downstream addresses. The attacker's rsETH collateral on Aave remains unredeemed, and the borrowed WETH and wstETH have not been returned; the lending positions have been abandoned.

... The causal chain of the KelpDAO incident spans three layers of the "decentralized" technology stack. It begins with a single point of dependence. KelpDAO's 1-of-1 DVN configuration reduced cross-chain verification to a single entity, making the entire bridge vulnerable to breach through a single compromised infrastructure component. The architecture supported decentralization, but the configuration did not. Composability then escalated a bridging vulnerability into a systemic liquidity crisis. An attack froze WETH, the most fundamental asset in DeFi, impacting five chains, affecting billions of dollars in liquidity, and involving users unrelated to rsETH or KelpDAO. The cascading impact was shaped by quantifiable parameters: aggressive LTV settings, shallow liquidity pools, and widespread cross-chain collateral deployment. The scale of the crisis forced decentralized governance to exercise centralized emergency powers. Arbitrum Security Council transferred 30,766 ETH without holder signatures through governance-authorized atomic contract upgrades. Aave froze markets on multiple chains through governance-driven emergency actions. These responses were effective, transparent, and arguably necessary, and also clear evidence that "permissionless" has real boundaries. Single point of dependence makes attacks possible, composability amplifies damage, and the damage reveals the centralized power that has always been embedded in upgradeable contracts and governance frameworks. Addressing these interconnected issues requires joint action from all parties involved: For protocol parties: The overall security level of a protocol depends on its weakest link, and in this case, the weakest link was the DVN infrastructure rather than smart contracts [10]. Effective security requires systematic coverage of multiple dimensions, including code security, infrastructure security, key management, and operational security. Comprehensive security assessments and penetration tests should stress test the entire technology stack, rather than examining isolated individual components. On-chain monitoring enables protocols to trigger emergency responses within minutes rather than hours, while rapid cross-chain fund tracing is crucial for coordinating asset freezes and maximizing recovery. Specifically for lending protocols, the collateral for cross-chain synthetic assets should be stress-tested for a "complete collateral collapse" scenario, combining the three parameters discussed above (LTV, pool depth, and number of cross-chain deployments). For L2 governance and DAOs: Emergency powers should be transparent and accountable. Most major L2s already possess this capability, but it is often buried deep in technical documentation rather than presented in user-facing materials. Governance frameworks should clearly codify trigger conditions, scope limitations, time constraints, and post-event accountability requirements. For users: Understand the systemic risks inherent in DeFi composability. In this incident, WETH depositors who had never used rsETH had their liquidity frozen across five chains. The risk of a single position is only part of the whole picture; the protocols, pools, collateral types, and chains your assets interact with collectively constitute an interconnected risk surface.

data.title: US military admits to operating Bitcoin nodes
data.description:
Author: Martin

On April 23, overseas media Decrypt reported that Admiral Samuel Paparow, Commander of the U.S. Pacific Command, revealed a development related to blockchain technology during a hearing of a relevant military committee of the U.S. Congress.According to him, the U.S. government is currently running a Bitcoin node. The core purpose of this node is to conduct network security testing, and it does not engage in any mining operations.

This information has drawn public attention to the official direction of technical exploration and security research on the underlying technology of blockchain.

data.title: "Mad King" Trump, "Crazy Wars," and "Mad Markets"
data.description:
On April 23, the US-Iran war entered its eighth week. Just days earlier, the situation had taken a turn for the better: the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon had been implemented, Iran announced the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, and the Islamabad negotiations seemed imminent. However, Trump subsequently announced that the US naval blockade would not be lifted and ordered the inspection of ships bound for Iran—Iran immediately announced the re-closure of the strait and firmly rejected a second round of negotiations. This kind of capriciousness is not the first time. From the start of the war until now, this conflict can always be described with one word: madness. A "mad king" president, ousted from the war room by his staff, waged an unsolvable war with reversals every hour, creating a runaway market that even mainstream media couldn't comprehend. And the latest revelations truly reveal where this "madness and loss of control" comes from, and where it will lead the situation. "Mad King" Trump: The President Locked Out. On April 22nd, according to the latest insider information leaked by local US media, an incident that occurred over this year's Easter weekend profoundly reveals the management style of this war. At that time, a US F-15 was shot down in Iranian airspace, and the two pilots were missing. When the news reached the White House, Trump yelled at his staff for hours. “The Europeans didn’t offer any help,” he repeatedly said. At that time, the average price of gasoline in the U.S. had risen to $4.09 a gallon, and images of the 1979 Iranian hostage crisis kept replaying in his mind. “You see Carter (the 39th U.S. president)… helicopters, hostages, that cost him the election,” Trump complained at the time. “It was a mess.” He demanded that the military immediately go to rescue the hostages. But his aides judged that his impatience wouldn't help at this crucial moment. So they kept the president outside the decision-making room, only going out to report to him on progress at key junctures. Vice President Vance connected via video from Camp David, White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles called from her home in Florida, and the entire team tracked the rescue progress almost minute by minute—the plane getting stuck in the sand, the diversionary tactic against Iranian forces…while the president could only wait outside for calls. One pilot was quickly found. The second pilot wasn't rescued until late Saturday night. Trump didn't go to sleep until after 2 a.m. Six hours later, on Easter morning, he posted the social media message that shocked the world: “Open the Fuckin' Strait, you crazy bastards, or you will live in hell.” He ended the post with an Islamic prayer. This post was not part of any national security plan. According to senior White House officials, it was an impromptu remark by Trump. He said he wanted to make himself “look as unstable and as offensive as possible” because he believed it was a language Iranians “understand.” After posting, he asked his staff, "How's the reaction?" From a collapsing figure consumed by fear to a mad strategist—Trump switched roles in 12 hours. The question is: Which one is the real him? Or are they both? International relations scholar John Mearsheimer used the term "mad king" in a recent interview. "A Frenzied War": Undermining Underlying Trust Between the US and Iran Under the sway of this extremely emotional sentiment, US diplomatic actions have regressed dramatically, directly leading to the breakdown of today's peace talks. Iran has repeatedly emphasized that it was the US's repeated threats and capriciousness that caused them to refuse the second round of negotiations. In his post-mortem analysis, Mearsheimer astutely pointed out that a crucial ceasefire window existed last Friday: when Iran had tentatively opened the Strait of Hormuz in goodwill, the US should have seized the opportunity to advance the Islamabad negotiations. However, the Trump administration at that moment unilaterally broke this tacit agreement: it not only publicly announced its refusal to lift the naval blockade against Iran but also ordered US forces to intercept, open fire on, and board and search Iranian vessels. "The result was that the Iranians made a 180-degree turn and closed the Strait again." This tactic, lacking strategic resolve and constantly shifting course at crucial moments, completely exhausted Washington's strategic credibility. In the eyes of Iranian hardliners, the United States has become a "madman" devoid of any sense of contractual obligation, rendering any negotiations meaningless. This complete collapse of trust directly pushed the peace talks to their doom. "A Mad Strategy": How Israel "Sells" War and "Controls" Trump The source of this loss of control lies in Washington's extremely rare practice of "outsourcing" its great power strategy to lobbying from external forces. International relations scholar John Mearsheimer stated that, with the exception of a very few, such as Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, most of the top U.S. military and intelligence officials held strong skepticism or even opposition to this war. They clearly anticipated the extremely high risks, including Iran's potential retaliation by blocking the Strait of Hormuz. But Trump completely ignored the warnings from his own experts. Mearsheimer bluntly stated: "The Israelis sold him a bill of goods." In the White House Situation Room, Mossad chief David Barnea and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu painted a vision for Trump: the overwhelming military power of the U.S. military would bring a swift and decisive victory, and there would be no need to worry about Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz. Obsessed with the experience of Venezuela's "bloodless regime change in hours," Trump readily paid the price. After the war began, Trump saw footage of explosions in Iran and clips of "victory videos" every morning. His aides described him as "shocked" by the scale of the military force, repeatedly praising the performance of the US military. However, this "impressive" performance on the battlefield did not translate into a political victory. As the war truly entered its deeper stages, the loss of strategic control began to emerge. On the one hand, facing the Strait of Hormuz blockade that cuts off 20% of the world's oil supply, Trump rejected the military's suggestion to send ground troops to seize Kharg Island (which supplies 90% of Iran's oil exports) because he was extremely afraid of unacceptable US casualties; on the other hand, Israel even bypassed the United States and directly attacked Iran's largest gas field, South Pars, forcing Trump to urgently distance himself from the situation on social media. This state of being strategically constrained and tactically hesitant to act will inevitably lead to the complete loss of control over the war. "The Madness of the Strait of Hormuz": A Problem No One Planned for" When top-level decision-makers are both unpredictable and influenced by external forces, bottom-level execution inevitably descends into chaos. The Strait of Hormuz is the best example. Before the war broke out, Trump told his team that the Iranian government was likely to yield on the Strait issue, and even if it didn't, the US military could handle it. However, when tanker traffic quickly came to a standstill after the bombing began, some White House advisors were caught off guard. Trump later expressed belated surprise: "A guy with a drone could just shut it down." This is the most ironic scene in the whole story: those who start a war don't think about what will happen afterward. Faced with this predicament of a lack of contingency plans at the highest levels regarding key issues, Jim Bianco, founder of market research firm Bianco Research, put it more bluntly at the Hedgeye Investment Summit on April 23: "My frustration lies in the fact that they have no plan whatsoever for the Strait of Hormuz, or rather, they have a plan but it's completely ineffective. What the market really cares about now is the flow of oil. The market can be patient with nuclear weapons; but it has no patience for oil flows." In this back-and-forth political game, Brent crude oil has broken through $102, completely reversing last week's decline and continuing to rise.

“A frenzied market”: “The crude oil pricing mechanism is paralyzed”

When political decisions lose their anchor, financial markets also lose their anchor.

When political decisions lose their anchor, financial markets also lose their anchor.

The first to collapse is the pricing mechanism of underlying commodities. Jim Bianco reveals an extremely dangerous signal: the pricing function of the global crude oil market has become dysfunctional. In normal years, the price spreads between Western Canadian Select, Brent, WTI, and spot Oman crude oil typically remain within a very narrow range of $1 to $2, a sign of a healthy global energy supply chain. But today, with the two-way blockade and the protracted "no-timetable" war, these spot crude oil price spreads have surged to a staggering $60! "If you're extremely bearish, you can find quotes at $70; if you're extremely bullish, there are physical quotes at $130." Bianco warned that this extreme dispersion proves the physical network of the crude oil market has been severed by geopolitics. Brent crude breaking through $102 is merely a symptom; the truly fatal flaw is that the underlying pricing anchor has vanished. In other words: nobody knows what oil is really worth. This isn't market volatility; it's market failure. However, facing the abyss of the real economy, the US financial market exhibited a frenzied "apocalyptic euphoria." The US stock market continued to reach new highs. Funds traded at high frequency based on Trump's emotional tweets, like chasing "meme stocks." The market blindly bought in at the slightest hint of positive news from the White House. Even amidst the stalemate in the war, Trump himself spent considerable time boasting to his financial backers about deserving the Medal of Honor and studying the interior design plans for the White House ballroom. But the illusory K-line charts cannot conceal the bleeding at the bottom. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index delivered the harshest verdict—this authoritative data point with a 74-year history plummeted to an unprecedented 47 points in March of this year. The level of despair among the American public regarding the current economy far surpasses that of the 2008 subprime mortgage crisis, the 9/11 terrorist attacks, and the Great Inflation of the 1970s. This is a starkly contrasting and completely out-of-control K-shaped macroeconomic picture: stock market bulls are toasting to the White House-manipulated news flow, while gasoline prices as high as $4.09 have shattered the survival line for ordinary people. Is Trump "manipulating" the market? This is the most sensitive and difficult question for market participants to discuss openly. Keith McCullough, speaking directly at the summit, echoed the sentiments of many: "Trump seems increasingly adept at manipulating market movements when and in the direction he wants, because people are still too focused on a single factor." He further pointed out that the correlation coefficient between the current dollar, oil prices, gold, and Bitcoin is approaching 95%. "It's not complicated," he said. "If you can know in advance where oil prices and the dollar are going, you can know the direction of almost all assets." Even more noteworthy was a detail he mentioned: Iran has begun releasing Lego memes mocking Trump for shorting oil every time he announces the Strait of Hormuz "is about to open." "It's an open secret," McCullough said. "And nobody seems to care because everyone wants the same thing—the market is going up, Trump is pulling it up, okay, keep going." The Real Risk of This Game Mearsheimer said something in an interview that's worth pondering: "The Trump administration should want a deal. There are two reasons: first, they can't win on the path of escalation; second, they risk pushing the global economy off a cliff. So they should want a deal." "But sometimes Trump acts like he wants a deal, and sometimes he acts like he doesn't." This is precisely the most dangerous aspect of the current situation—not the deliberate destruction by either side, but a systemic loss of control driven by chaotic decision-making. Trump, while hesitant to actually send ground troops to seize Halke Island, has been issuing increasingly strong threats on social media, even sending contradictory signals when his aides attempt to control the situation. In this "game of chicken," both sides are waiting for the other to blink first. The problem is that when one side's decision-makers are themselves in an unpredictable state, no one can truly calculate where the Nash equilibrium of this game lies. Once the "out-of-control" gears start turning, they are difficult to stop in the short term.

data.title: After its market value plummeted by 97%, iQiyi plans to use AI to forcibly prolong its life.
data.description:
At the iQiyi World Conference in April 2026, Gong Yu stood on stage and said that live-action filming will eventually become an intangible cultural heritage. The large screen on stage displayed the "AI Artist Library," with virtual avatars tirelessly changing expressions and costumes. Gong Yu said that iQiyi has signed more than 100 AI artists. Because of the use of AI, shooting time will be greatly shortened, and a large amount of post-production work can be completed by digital avatars. He also announced the official commercial launch of "Nadou Pro," an AIGC film and television production platform for small and medium-sized creators, providing AI tools, new revenue-sharing regulations, and a "no upper limit" revenue commitment. This press conference was packaged as a technology carnival, with music, lighting, and PPT all supporting this narrative: the future is here, embrace it. Beyond this grand event, another large screen displayed a number: $1.41. This was iQiyi's closing price on Nasdaq, with a market capitalization of only $1.361 billion, just one step away from delisting. When it went public in 2018, iQiyi's stock price reached a high of $46, with a peak market capitalization exceeding $31.2 billion. From $31.2 billion to $1.3 billion, it plummeted by 97% in less than eight years. While the outside world debates whether AI can perform well, the capital market is only concerned with whether AI can tell a good story. Over the past decade, iQiyi has burned through hundreds of billions of yuan, yet it has still not found a path to sustainable profitability. In 2025, the content costs of this streaming giant still reached a staggering 15.45 billion yuan, accounting for 56% of its total annual revenue; its net loss for the year was 206.3 million yuan, turning from profit to loss; and its operating profit was only 640 million yuan, a sharp drop of 73% compared to 2.36 billion yuan in 2024. Just two years prior, in 2023, iQiyi's net profit reached a peak of 1.93 billion yuan, the highest since the company's inception. From peak to loss, it only took two years. Even more problematic is that iQiyi's free cash flow has plummeted from over 3.3 billion yuan in 2023 to less than 10 million yuan, indicating a rapid decline in its self-financing ability. The company issued convertible bonds twice in 2023 and 2025, totaling nearly $1 billion, further increasing its interest burden. With its US stock market financing function essentially rendered ineffective, iQiyi secretly submitted its listing application to the Hong Kong Stock Exchange at the end of March 2026, and in April it unveiled its AI artist database and the "NaDou Pro" project. A company seeking to "replenish its capital" through a Hong Kong listing needs a new narrative that can catch investors' eye. "Marginal costs approaching zero" and "AI cost reduction and efficiency improvement"—these terms are more valuable in today's capital market context than any hit drama. This has nothing to do with art; it's all about keeping the company listed. But when a company no longer relies on content quality but instead on "cost reduction" to please investors, who will be the first to fuel this cost-cutting machine? The focus of public opinion surrounding this AI controversy was quickly dominated by several lawyer's letters. Zhang Ruoyun was the first to speak out, stating that he had never authorized any platform to generate an AI avatar and reserved the right to pursue legal action. Yu Hewei, Chen Xiao, Wang Churan, and others followed suit, all denying the rumors. Yi Yangqianxi's studio issued a statement pointing out that several AI short dramas had used his image and voice without authorization, with the short dramas in question reaching nearly 75 million views. Actors such as Tan Jianci, Gong Jun, and Yang Zi also spoke out to protect their rights. Suddenly, the "AI Artist Database" became a hot potato, forcing iQiyi to issue an explanation, stating that the AI ​​artist database only contains "authorized AI artists," not digital copies of real celebrities, and that "artists joining the NaDou Pro artist database only indicates their interest in engaging in AI film and television projects." Gong Yu also responded publicly multiple times, emphasizing that the platform respects actors' rights and that AI is a tool, not a replacement.

While a big star's statement can trend on social media, there are still 134,000 extras in Hengdian World Studios, out of the public eye.

In the past few years, with the explosion of the short drama market, the demand for extras in Hengdian has rebounded somewhat. Short dramas have many production teams, short production cycles, and fast pace, resulting in a large demand for extras.

In the past few years, with the explosion of the short drama market, the demand for extras in Hengdian World Studios has rebounded somewhat. Short dramas have many production teams, short production cycles, and fast pace, resulting in a large demand for extras.

In long dramas, extras are just background figures, without lines or names; in short dramas, they have the opportunity to appear on screen, say a few lines, and even play main roles. An average extra earns between 150 and 300 yuan per day, and with hard work, they can earn four or five thousand yuan a month. Not much, but enough to live on. However, with the arrival of AI, this "enough to live on" has begun to waver. In the first quarter of 2026, over 85% of short drama production crews in Hengdian had fully switched to using AI actors for supporting roles and extras, and the employment rate of full-time actors in live-action short dramas plummeted from 82% in 2025 to 29%. Some production crews no longer recruit extras; they only need to film the main characters in front of a green screen, and the remaining background crowd is generated by algorithms with a single click. AI-generated extras don't need to eat, rest, are never late, don't ask for raises, and never complain. Hengdian has approximately 134,000 registered extras, but only about 800 effective job openings are available daily, making the competition even fiercer than taking the civil service exam. One actor once maintained a pace of filming more than 20 days a month, shooting hundreds of short dramas alone, but after the Spring Festival, he didn't film a single day for over a month. After this year's Spring Festival, the production volume of live-action short dramas plummeted by 50%. Big stars defend their portrait rights on trending topics, while small extras disappear under the onslaught of AI. Top stars have management companies, lawyers, and trending topics on Weibo; their rights protection efforts can become news, create public pressure, and force platforms to make concessions. But extras have nothing. Their disappearance won't be news; it will only become the ever-shorter waiting lines outside some film crew in Hengdian. The hymns of technological revolution are always written with the sighs of the working class. But in this cost-cutting game, what role do the platforms themselves play? Are they truly driving a technological revolution, or are they using a beautiful name to shirk their heaviest burdens? From "Landlord" to "Payroll Pump" iQiyi announced at the conference that it would transform into a "decentralized social media," launching "NaDou Pro" and modifying its revenue-sharing rules. "NaDou Pro" is positioned as a content platform for small and medium-sized creators, providing AI creation tools, supporting users to create their own short dramas, and earning revenue through revenue sharing. Gong Yu said this is "returning the power of content creation to the public," a "decentralized" content revolution. The new revenue-sharing rules promise creators "unlimited" revenue sharing, and AIGC content and Chinese drama content will also receive additional subsidies and incentives. This sounds like empowering creators and handing the stage over to the public. But it's only "sounds like it." Long-form video platforms used to be "landlords" who poured money into buying dramas. At its peak, iQiyi's annual content acquisition and production expenditure exceeded 20 billion yuan, with a single S-level drama often costing hundreds of millions of yuan, and the platform bearing its own profits and losses. The logic of this model was: I spend money, I buy content, I attract user subscriptions, I earn membership fees. In 2023, iQiyi's membership service revenue reached 20.3 billion yuan, and its membership scale exceeded 120 million, marking the company's peak. However, this logic has a fatal flaw: it assumes that the platform can continuously judge what constitutes good content, and that good content can consistently bring in a sufficient number of subscribers. iQiyi has invested heavily for ten years, losing money for ten years. Its membership numbers have declined steadily from a peak of 120 million, with membership service revenue dropping to 16.81 billion yuan in 2025, a year-on-year decrease of approximately 5%. Even more problematic is that one of iQiyi's cost-cutting measures is the vigorous promotion of a film revenue-sharing model, where the platform no longer prepays copyright fees but shares revenue with partners based on actual viewership. This does reduce upfront cash expenditure, but the side effect is that the film studios willing to accept revenue sharing are mostly mid-tier content creators; for truly popular IPs that can drive membership subscriptions, copyright holders still prefer to buy them outright at high prices. As a result, iQiyi's content library was filled with mid-tier works that prioritized quantity over quality, lacking truly blockbuster hits. Users noticed this, and platform stickiness and willingness to pay declined accordingly. This model has reached its end. Now, iQiyi doesn't want to lose money; it wants to make a guaranteed profit. The platform is handing out AI tools to countless individual creators, letting them experiment and learn from their mistakes. Whoever becomes popular, the platform takes the lion's share; whoever flops, the platform remains unaffected. Just as Uber doesn't hire drivers and Airbnb doesn't buy properties, iQiyi doesn't want to buy dramas anymore. This logic has a more academic name in the internet industry: "platform capitalism." Platforms provide infrastructure, attracting a large number of suppliers, and then transfer the risk to the suppliers through algorithmic distribution and commission mechanisms, keeping the profits for themselves. YouTube and TikTok are successful examples of this logic. However, the success of these two platforms is based on the premise that they themselves are traffic portals and already have a huge user base. Can iQiyi replicate this logic? This is a questionable question. iQiyi's monthly active user base is not on the same scale as TikTok's; its content consumption scenario is "sitting down to watch dramas," not "scrolling." If a paid subscription platform is transformed into a UGC content platform, why would users stay? But what's even more worthy of inquiry is what does it mean for the entire content industry when long-form video platforms start lowering themselves to act as "pumps" and transferring all the risks of content production to individual creators? What are those short-form video platforms, which originally relied on short, quick, and easy content, doing now? Two Routes While iQiyi is betting on AI, Douyin is investing 500 million yuan to support "live-action short dramas." On April 15, 2026, at the 13th China Internet Audio-Visual Conference, Douyin Group released its latest live-action short drama support plan, with the platform investing 500 million yuan in special funds to continuously support content innovation and in-depth development of realistic themes in live-action short dramas. At the press conference, Le Li, editor-in-chief of Hongguo Short Drama, clearly stated that live-action short dramas have significant advantages in user retention, completion rate, and long-term willingness to pay. Douyin's 500 million yuan support plan specifically targets "live-action short dramas," explicitly excluding AI-generated content. Douyin's logic is that AI-generated content on the platform has become rampant, leading to user fatigue; real faces, genuine emotional expression, and authentic body language have become scarce commodities. Live-action short dramas contribute the vast majority of the platform's views, favorites, interactions, and paid conversions, while AI-generated animated dramas lag significantly behind live-action content in user retention and completion rates. After short video platforms reach their traffic peak, the core issues they face are user retention and paid conversion. Low-quality AI content can fill timelines, but it cannot build user stickiness, let alone drive payment. Only genuine emotional resonance can achieve this. So, Douyin started spending heavily to acquire a "human touch." Meanwhile, long-form video platforms, which should have represented high-quality, in-depth content, jumped into the cheapest industrial assembly line due to financial desperation. This misalignment represents a rational choice under two different financial circumstances. Backed by ByteDance, Douyin has ample cash flow to make long-term investments in content quality; iQiyi's cash reserves are insufficient to support the long-term gamble of high-cost content, forcing it to find a "low-cost, high-volume" path. In contrast, Tencent Video, backed by Tencent Holdings, saw its parent company's revenue reach 557.3 billion yuan in the first three quarters of 2025, a year-on-year increase of 14%. Tencent Video maintains its leading position in the long-form video market with over 340 million monthly active users and 114 million members. Mango TV, relying on Hunan Broadcasting System's content supply chain and low-cost self-production capabilities, maintains stable profitability. iQiyi, lacking both Tencent's social ecosystem and Mango's cost advantage, faces the most difficult situation among the three major video platforms. However, the path of "low cost, high output" has never been a good one in the content industry. Data shows that the proportion of AI-generated humanoid short dramas in the top 100 animation drama rankings has surged from 7% in 2025 to 38%, with thousands of new AI short dramas being authorized for broadcast daily. A small team of three, a few thousand yuan in computing power costs, and 48 hours to produce a drama—that's the production efficiency of AI short dramas. However, in this sector, 90% of companies are operating at a loss, and over 60% of the works have dismal viewership and struggle to generate profits. Of the tens of thousands of online works, only a handful have achieved over 100 million views. Low barriers to entry are never a competitive advantage; they only make the market overcrowded. On October 6, 1927, the first sound film, *The Jazz Singer*, premiered in New York. Before this, every movie theater had a band specifically for accompanying silent films. Larger theaters had bands of twenty or thirty musicians, while smaller theaters had at least one pianist. They improvised based on the plot on screen, playing somber strings for tragedies and light piano for comedies. It was a craft, and also a stable livelihood. There were approximately 22,000 theater musicians across the United States; they had their own unions, their own industry standards, and their own professional dignity. After the advent of sound films, almost all of these musicians lost their jobs within a few years. Between 1927 and 1930, the number of cinema musicians in the United States plummeted from approximately 22,000 to less than 5,000. Their unions launched a boycott, organizing musicians to refuse to play in cinemas equipped with sound equipment, but to no avail. The tide of technology does not stop because of opposition. John Philip Sousa wrote "The Threat of Mechanical Music" as early as 1906, predicting that the phonograph and mechanical instruments would replace live performances, turning music into an industrial product and depriving performers of their livelihoods. Today, Gong Yu's statement that "live-action films become intangible cultural heritage" seems at first glance to be the same thing film companies said to cinema musicians back then. Every technological revolution needs an "intangible cultural heritage" narrative to complete the handover, beautifying the obsolete as "precious traditions," and then conveniently sweeping them into museums. The term "intangible cultural heritage" itself is a form of appeasement; it acknowledges your existence and value, but also declares the end of your era.

Sound films and AI artist databases seem similar on the surface, but there's a fundamental difference.

Sound films are a new form of expression. They allow films to tell more complex stories, express richer emotions, and ultimately give audiences more.

Theatrical musicians have lost their jobs, but the art form of film itself has become more complete and powerful.

What is an AI artist database? It's not a new form of expression; it's a cheaper way of replication. It doesn't make film and television more complete; it just makes them cheaper. The logic of sound films is to use better technology to tell better stories and attract more people to theaters. The logic of AI artist databases is to produce more content at a lower cost and spend less money.

This is not the same thing. If iQiyi's AI bet succeeds, and it wins, what about the audience? Stimulation replaces empathy, reality is folded. The ultimate outcome of the proliferation of AI-generated films and television shows is not that they replace anything, but rather that they subtly alter the mechanisms by which humans receive stories and perceive genuine emotions. Neuroscience research shows that when humans watch live performances, mirror neurons in the brain are activated, generating emotional resonance. We can, to some extent, feel the emotions that the actors are experiencing. This is an ability that humans have evolved over tens of thousands of years, the foundation for our understanding of others and the establishment of social connections. This is why a good movie or TV series can linger long after it ends, even changing a person's perspective on certain things. AI-generated performances can achieve high realism technically, but they lack genuine experience. An AI-generated character cries because the algorithm calculated that the scene required crying, not because the character actually experienced something. The human brain can perceive this difference to some extent, which is why AI-generated content has a much lower completion rate and paid conversion rate than live-action content, even if its visuals are more refined and the plot more "exciting." However, this perceptual ability is subject to disuse. When a generation grows up accustomed to the absolutely smooth, high-frequency stimulation provided by AI, their brains gradually adapt to this stimulation pattern, and their threshold for perceiving complex, subtle, and nuanced real emotions increases. Just as someone who eats strongly flavored food for a long time gradually loses the ability to appreciate the taste of bland food. McLuhan said, "The medium is a massage," meaning that the medium is not merely a conduit for delivering content; it shapes the way we perceive the world. When the logic of content production shifts from "telling a true story" to "using algorithms to calculate the stimulation the audience wants," the medium ceases to be a massage and becomes anesthesia. This isn't a lament for "live-action" films, nor is it saying that AI is inherently bad. Technology itself is neither good nor evil; the problem lies in what it's used for and under what business logic it's widely adopted. A company betting on AI out of financial desperation and a creator who genuinely believes AI can improve art use the same technology, but with completely different motivations, and the results will be entirely different. Gong Yu said that AI can enable actors to take on four projects a year instead of two. The subtext of this statement is that an actor's value lies not in what they experience on set, but in how many times their face is rendered. But true acting is never about the face. It's about a person making an unpredictable choice at a specific moment, due to a real experience. AI can't calculate that. However, once audiences become accustomed to being fed by algorithms, will they still need something that can't be calculated?
